claim: In older adults, high-contrast visual acuity is generally preserved, while visual performance under reduced contrast, reduced luminance, glare, and divided-attention conditions is substantially impaired.
support_summary: Several studies in older adults indicate that standard/high-contrast acuity is less affected than more demanding visual functions: high-contrast acuity and standard visual fields had the lowest failure rates, whereas nonstandard tests involving illumination, contrast, glare, and attentional load failed more often (PMID: 12028258). Multiple population-based and functional studies further show that contrast sensitivity, glare sensitivity, low-luminance vision, and visual attention/UFOV are important predictors of disability, crashes, or night driving performance, often beyond what standard acuity explains (PMID: 11133849, 17389475, 28564700, 35966995, 7898882). Experimental and clinical studies also report that older adults show greater disability glare than younger adults and that glare/low luminance can markedly worsen low-contrast or mesopic performance even when standard acuity is relatively good or only minimally impaired (PMID: 1787947, 8594525, 8942376, 1497520). Cataract-related and simulation studies consistently emphasize the mismatch between preserved or near-preserved acuity and poorer contrast/glare-dependent real-world function (PMID: 8942376, 1497520, 10198867). Some support items are only indirect or not specific to aging populations, but the recurring theme is that standard acuity alone underestimates age-related impairment under reduced contrast, reduced luminance, glare, and attentionally demanding conditions.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly comparative rather than directly testing normal aging effects. In older adults with multifocal versus monofocal intraocular lenses, mesopic/glare-related test differences were observed, yet simulator task performance did not differ significantly between groups, suggesting that reduced-contrast or glare deficits do not always translate into substantially impaired complex task performance (PMID: 30123845). Another small paired-eye IOL study found only intermediate-frequency contrast sensitivity loss and minimal glare-related acuity differences, concluding there was no dramatic reduction in visual functions versus monofocal lenses (PMID: 8152190). However, both studies concern implanted lens designs rather than the general visual aging trajectory, so their contradiction to the claim is only partial.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The support comes from multiple older-adult and functional studies showing that standard/high-contrast acuity is relatively less affected, while contrast, low-luminance, glare, and divided-attention-related performance are more vulnerable; the contradictory evidence is narrower, device-specific, and less directly applicable to normal aging.

claim: In older adults, stereopsis, color discrimination, and peripheral visual field function are often markedly reduced.
support_summary: Several studies suggest age-related declines in parts of the claimed visual triad, though support is uneven across domains. A study comparing older versus middle-aged adults found significant decrements in stereopsis and color vision in the older group (PMID: 8355971), and another study in healthy older adults linked reduced stereopsis with poorer inhibitory control, implying that reduced stereoacuity is present in a subset of older adults (PMID: 33324156). Evidence for peripheral visual function comes from a functional field of view study showing older adults made more errors than younger adults across conditions (PMID: 28114486), and a dementia study reporting self-reported impairment in color discrimination and peripheral vision among older adults with dementia (PMID: 35275536). One support item is less directly relevant because it concerns Parkinson's disease rather than older adults generally, though it does show worse stereopsis and color perception in that disease group (PMID: 24886673).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited but includes one aging study directly suggesting preserved color-related performance despite other age effects. In a cross-sectional perimetry study, elderly participants had narrower object-detection visual fields than controls, but angular thresholds for color detection were not significantly different, arguing against a general age-related reduction in color discrimination/color visual field detection (PMID: 34918686). Another cited contradiction appears mismatched to the claim because it studied multiple system atrophy patients rather than older adults broadly, and found normal color discrimination despite retinal structural abnormalities (PMID: 26359930). Overall, contradiction mainly targets the color-discrimination component and does not strongly refute stereopsis or peripheral visual field decline in older adults generally.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. Support comes from multiple studies showing age-related reductions in stereopsis, color vision, and functional/peripheral visual performance, although some evidence is indirect or limited to subgroups, while the main contradiction is a single aging study showing preserved color-detection thresholds rather than broad preservation across all claimed functions.

support_highlights.snippet: We found that the total error scores (TESs) and partial error scores (PESs) for red, green, blue and purple were all significantly higher in PD patients than in control patients.

claim: Across multiple spatial vision measures in older adults, visual performance declines with age at a similar overall rate, although different visual functions begin to decline at different ages.
support_summary: Support is limited and somewhat mixed. An overview of the SKI longitudinal study in older adults reports a direct comparison of age-related declines across vision functions, but states that "the rates of declines with advancing age vary widely for the different vision functions," which actually conflicts with the claim of a similar overall rate while still supporting that different functions age differently (PMID: 11384002). A lifespan visual-field study found that some measures worsened with age whereas reaction time remained nearly constant until later life, again suggesting different onset patterns of decline rather than a common rate across measures (PMID: 22484795). A survey study across adulthood also found multiple visual dimensions declined with age, but explicitly said varying rates of decline suggest different aspects of vision age at different rates, which only partially aligns with the claim's second clause and not its first (PMID: 3361091).
contradict_summary: There are no formally provided contradicting evidence items. However, within the cited support set, two abstracts explicitly indicate that decline rates differ across visual functions rather than showing a similar overall rate, creating an internal discrepancy with the claim (PMID: 11384002; PMID: 3361091).
conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported overall. Available studies support that different visual functions begin or progress in decline at different ages, but the abstracts do not clearly support—and in some cases implicitly challenge—the assertion that multiple spatial vision measures decline at a similar overall rate.

support_highlights.snippet: Here we summarize a few of the findings to date, with an emphasis on a direct comparison of declines in different aspects of vision function with age.
The rates of declines with advancing age vary widely for the different vision functions.
DPR and perimetric thresholds increased with eccentricity and age; the periphery showed a more pronounced age-related increase than the center.
RT increased only slightly and uniformly with eccentricity.
It remained almost constant up to the age of 60, a marked change occurring only above 80.
The surveys revealed five dimensions that declined with increasing age: visual processing speed, light sensitivity, dynamic vision, near vision, and visual search.
The percentage of respondents reporting a decline in these visual dimensions increased two- to sixfold across the adult life span.
Varying rates of visual decline for different tasks suggest that various aspects of vision age at different rates.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, myopia prevalence increased with age during childhood and adolescence, reaching very high levels in older adolescents.
support_summary: A large nationwide Taiwan survey directly supports the claim, showing myopia prevalence rose steeply with age from 20% at age 7 to 61% at 12, 81% at 15, and 84% at ages 16-18, with substantial high myopia in 18-year-olds (PMID: 11760374). A Taiwan suburban school study also reported significant associations between myopia and age, concluding that myopia started early and progressively worsened over the school years, though the abstract gives fewer age-specific prevalence figures and only includes ages 6-15 (PMID: 30120433). One additional study found the same age-related increase in Xi'an, China rather than Taiwan, so it is only indirect contextual support and also represents a population mismatch relative to the claim (PMID: 36585663). Overall, the main recurring theme is a strong age-related rise in both prevalence and severity, reaching very high levels by later adolescence in Taiwan, especially in the nationwide survey.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall well supported. The strongest evidence is the large nationwide Taiwan study directly documenting a marked age-related increase in myopia prevalence to very high levels in older adolescents, while the other studies are consistent but either less specific or from a different population.

support_highlights.snippet: The myopia rate increased from 20% at 7 years, to 61% at 12 years, and 81% at 15 years.
A myopic rate of 84% was found for schoolchildren aged 16 years through 18 years.
The prevalence of high myopia (> -6.0 D) at the age of 18 years was 24% in girls and 18% in boys.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, girls had a higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia than boys.
support_summary: Direct support comes from a large nationwide Taiwan survey showing that girls had both higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia than boys, with mean refraction at age 18 of -4.12 D in girls versus -3.15 D in boys and high myopia prevalence of 24% versus 18% (PMID: 11760374). Additional Taiwan evidence supports the prevalence component: a national interview survey found women under 40 had significantly higher myopia rates than men, though it did not focus specifically on schoolchildren or severity (PMID: 22344187), and a Hualien schoolchildren study reported higher myopia prevalence in girls than boys without severity data (PMID: 24138825). Two China-based school studies also found higher prevalence in girls and, in one case, higher moderate/high myopia in girls, but these are indirect because they are not from Taiwan (PMID: 28606071; PMID: 35392469). Overall, the strongest and most directly relevant evidence is the Taiwan nationwide survey, while the remaining studies mainly reinforce the prevalence pattern and only partly address severity or the Taiwan-specific population.
contradict_summary: One preschool study from Xuzhou, China reported that myopia prevalence was higher in boys than girls (PMID: 37678562). However, this evidence is only weakly contradictory to the claim because it concerns preschool children rather than schoolchildren in Taiwan and does not address severity. Thus, it suggests that sex patterns may differ by age or setting, but it does not directly rebut the Taiwan schoolchildren claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. This is driven by a directly on-point nationwide Taiwan study that explicitly found both higher prevalence and greater severity of myopia in girls, whereas the lone contradictory study is from a different country, younger age group, and lacks severity data.

support_highlights.snippet: The mean refractive index reached myopic status at the age of 8, and increased to -4.12 D in girls and -3.15 D in boys at the age of 18 years.
The prevalence of high myopia (> -6.0 D) at the age of 18 years was 24% in girls and 18% in boys.
Girls had a higher prevalence and more severe degree of myopia than boys.
Women had significantly higher rates of myopia than men for persons younger than 40 years of age (P<0.001).
Girls had a higher prevalence of dental caries, myopia, and hematuria than boys (all p < 0.01), whereas boys had a higher prevalence of pinworm than girls (p = 0.02).
Girls were more likely than boys to have myopia (odds ratio, 1.43 [95% CI, 1.14-1.96]), especially moderate myopia, and the prevalence of moderate and high myopia were higher in girls than in boys.
The total prevalence of myopia was significantly higher in girl than in boy participants.
The prevalence of myopia among girls was higher than that among boys (2019: 50.4 vs. 44.0%; 2020: 54.0 vs. 47.6%).

contradict_highlights.snippet: The prevalence was higher in boys than girls and in urban versus rural areas.

claim: Among schoolchildren in Taiwan, greater myopia severity was associated with longer axial length, and the study authors concluded that the rising prevalence and severity of high myopia may be related to earlier onset of myopia.
support_summary: Direct support comes primarily from a nationwide Taiwan survey showing that among schoolchildren, "the increase in axial length corresponded with the progression of myopia," and that prevalence/severity worsened versus 1995, especially in younger age groups; the authors concluded that preventing myopia at a young age may slow increasing severity, closely matching the claim (PMID: 11760374). Additional studies in children or young adults consistently report that more myopic refraction/high myopia is associated with longer axial length or related ocular elongation (PMID: 12187824, PMID: 17804923, PMID: 23322575, PMID: 24223862, PMID: 38110469), though several are not in Taiwanese schoolchildren and therefore are only indirect support. Several pediatric cohort studies also support the earlier-onset component, finding that younger age of myopia onset predicts more severe myopia/high myopia and longer axial length later (PMID: 27350183, PMID: 30120433, PMID: 37166795), again mostly outside the exact source population. Another Chinese school cohort found higher myopia incidence in children with longer axial length and emphasized prevention before school age to reduce onset of myopia/high myopia (PMID: 34989760). Overall, the support is thematically consistent, but only PMID: 11760374 directly addresses both the Taiwan schoolchild population and the authors' conclusion about earlier onset driving greater severity.
contradict_summary: One orthokeratology clinic study reported that among myopic children wearing OK lenses, greater baseline myopic refractive error was associated with smaller subsequent axial length growth during treatment (PMID: 28419129). This does not directly show that more severe myopia is associated with shorter absolute axial length; rather, it concerns reduced axial elongation over follow-up under an intervention, so its placement as strong contradiction is somewhat discrepant with the abstract. The study also does not address Taiwanese schoolchildren broadly or the claim that rising prevalence/severity of high myopia may relate to earlier onset. Thus, contradicting evidence is limited and only indirectly relevant.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported. A directly relevant Taiwan schoolchildren study (PMID: 11760374) supports both the axial-length/severity association and the authors' inference that increasing severity may relate to younger onset, while the lone contradictory item is indirect and appears to address treatment-related axial growth rather than absolute axial length or population prevalence.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, smoking and hypertension were associated with a higher likelihood of age-related macular degeneration, supporting smoking avoidance and hypertension prevention as potential strategies to reduce risk.
support_summary: Several studies support smoking as a risk factor for age-related macular degeneration (AMD), including a cohort of 60- to 80-year-olds in Copenhagen where smokers who inhaled had a 2.5-fold higher risk of atrophic maculopathy (PMID: 1557977), the Beaver Dam Eye Study linking current smoking to exudative macular degeneration (PMID: 8452123), and reviews stating cigarette smoking is among the most consistent AMD risk factors (PMID: 19027484). Additional evidence supports smoking avoidance as a preventive strategy, including a modeling study predicting fewer AMD cases and blindness after smoking cessation (PMID: 18783631) and an older-women cohort where smoking increased early AMD risk in those aged 80+ (PMID: 19796757). Support for hypertension is present but less consistent: NHANES data associated systolic blood pressure and past hypertension with AMD prevalence (PMID: 3421236), an Argentine case-control study reported odds ratios of 2.1 for hypertension and 2.2 for smoking (PMID: 23828466), and a review listed untreated hypertension and smoking as basic risk factors (PMID: 21137473). Some supporting items are indirect or nonspecific to the AREDS cohort, and one review-style article gives broad assertions without detailed effect estimates (PMID: 21137473). Overall, the support side shows a recurring theme that smoking is a robust modifiable AMD risk factor, while hypertension has more limited and heterogeneous support.
contradict_summary: Two studies weaken the claim specifically for hypertension and, in one case, for smoking. A Brazilian cross-sectional study found no association of AMD with either hypertension (p=0.1985) or smoking (p=0.6374), directly conflicting with the claim (PMID: 15905946). An Iranian elderly cohort found smoking strongly associated with AMD but reported no association with hypertension, so this study contradicts only the hypertension component while actually supporting the smoking component despite being placed in the contradict group, indicating a categorization discrepancy (PMID: 26644880). The main contradictory theme is therefore inconsistency for hypertension rather than smoking. These contradictions also come from non-AREDS populations, limiting direct applicability to the specific claim context.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence for smoking as an AMD risk factor is repeated across multiple studies and designs, whereas evidence for hypertension is mixed, with some supportive studies but notable null findings, so the smoking component is strong but the hypertension component is only moderately supported.

support_highlights.snippet: Among smokers who inhaled, the risk of atrophic maculopathy was 2.5 times higher than among non-smokers (P less than 0.01), whereas the use of hypnotics increased the risk of exudative maculopathy by 3.4 times (P less than 0.01).
BACKGROUND: Tobacco smoking is a risk factor for age-related macular degeneration, but studies of ex-smokers suggest quitting can reduce the risk.
RESULTS: If 1,000 smokers quit, our model predicted 48 fewer cases of macular degeneration, 12 fewer cases of blindness, and a gain of 1,600 QALYs.
CONCLUSION: Smoking cessation is unequivocally cost-effective in terms of its impact on age-related macular degeneration outcomes alone.
The most consistent risk factors associated with this ocular condition are increasing age and cigarette smoking.

There was an increased risk of early AMD among subjects aged 80 years or older who were smoking compared to those younger than 80 years who were not smoking (OR, 5.49; 95% CI, 1.57 to 19.20; P for interaction = .026).
CONCLUSIONS: The magnitude of the greater-than-additive effect of smoking on the age-adjusted risk of AMD reinforces recommendations to quit smoking even for older individuals.
The basic risk factors of the disease are smoking, untreated hypertension, atherosclerosis, cataract surgery, and appearance of the disease in the family.
Other factors included hypertension (OR: 2.1), smoking (OR: 2.2), and being of the Mestizo race, which lowered the risk of age-related macular degeneration (OR: 0.40).
PURPOSE: Smoking has been identified as a major modifiable risk factor for age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

Stratified analysis, adjusting for age, showed that education, systolic blood pressure, past history of hypertension, cerebrovascular disease, and refractive error were all associated with macular degeneration.
With the exception of education, these factors remained statistically significant when simultaneously entered into a logistic regression model.

The relative odds for exudative macular degeneration, one form of late age-related maculopathy, in females who were current smokers was 2.50 (95% confidence interval 1.01-6.20) compared with those who were ex-smokers or never smokers; for males, it was 3.29 (95% confidence interval 1.03-10.50).
These results suggest that exudative macular degeneration is associated with cigarette smoking and that different forms of macular degeneration may have different etiologies.

contradict_highlights.snippet: No age-related macular degeneration association with race (p=0.1367), hypertension (p=0.1985), diabetes (p=0.6712), smoking (p=0.6374), alcohol intake (p=0.7359), ocular sunlight exposure (p=0.9399), light-colored iris (p=0.1691), hyperopia (p=0.5098) and the usage of antioxidants (p=0.8251) was observed.
There was a significant association between AMD and smoking (P<0.001) but no association was seen with AMD and age, level of education, history of hyperlipidemia, hypertension and diabetes.
Multiple LRAs revealed that smoking increased AMD by odds ratio of 5.03 (95% confidence interval 2.47-10.23 p<0.001) as compared to nonsmokers.
CONCLUSION: According to our findings, the prevalence of AMD was relatively high and smoking increased the risk of AMD in the elderly population.

claim: In participants aged 60 to 80 years with age-related macular degeneration in the Age-Related Eye Disease Study, hyperopia, lens opacities, less education, female sex, increased body mass index, and white race were also associated with age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: One supporting study found that lens opacifying disease was associated with increased odds of age-related macular degeneration, and this association persisted after adjustment for age, sex, and systolic blood pressure, supporting the lens opacity component of the claim (PMID: 2729473). Another study evaluating AMD risk factors reported that increased body mass index was considered a possible risk factor worth further study, but its abstract does not clearly establish significant associations for hyperopia, lens opacities, female sex, education, or white race despite listing them among evaluated variables (PMID: 9577116). Overall, the supporting evidence mainly aligns with lens opacities and, more weakly, higher BMI, rather than the full set of factors in the claim. There is also a scope discrepancy because these studies are not specifically the AREDS 60-80-year participant analysis described in the claim.
contradict_summary: A cross-sectional Brazilian study found positive associations between AMD and female gender, cataract, cataract surgery, and obesity, which is partly concordant with the claim, but it explicitly reported no association with race or hyperopia (PMID: 15905946). This directly weakens the claim's assertions regarding white race and hyperopia, while not addressing education and only indirectly relating cataract to lens opacity. Because the abstract contains both supportive and contradictory findings relative to the claim, this evidence is mixed but overall partially contradictory. The population and setting also differ from the AREDS cohort, limiting direct comparability.
conclusion: The statement is only partially supported overall and remains unclear based on the provided evidence. Support exists mainly for lens opacities and possibly higher BMI, but a contradictory study explicitly found no association with hyperopia or race, and none of the cited abstracts directly confirms the full AREDS-specific combination of hyperopia, less education, female sex, increased BMI, white race, and lens opacities.

support_highlights.snippet: Data from 3,087 persons age 45 or older in the National Health and Nutrition Survey, 1971-74, showed that subjects with lens opacifying disease had an increased odds for age-related macular degeneration (AMD) compared to those who had no lens opacities.
The association remained after controlling for age, sex, and systolic blood pressure (a common risk factor) in a logistic regression model.

The following risk factors were evaluated: age, sex, body mass index, history of general medical conditions, cigarette smoking, sun exposure, family history of AMD and ocular conditions such as iris color, lens opacities, hyperopia, gerontoxon and changes in retinal vessels.

CONCLUSIONS: The studies confirmed the role of age in the development of AMD and indicated cardiovascular disturbances, increased body mass index and light iris as the possible risk factors for AMD that are most worth further studying.

contradict_highlights.snippet: This association was also present in the female gender (p=0.0451), the presence of cataract (p=0.0447), cataract surgery (p=0.0432) and obesity (p<0.0001).

No age-related macular degeneration association with race (p=0.1367), hypertension (p=0.1985), diabetes (p=0.6712), smoking (p=0.6374), alcohol intake (p=0.7359), ocular sunlight exposure (p=0.9399), light-colored iris (p=0.1691), hyperopia (p=0.5098) and the usage of antioxidants (p=0.8251) was observed.
No age-related macular degeneration association with socioeconomic level was found in this study.

claim: In patients with subfoveal choroidal neovascularization caused by age-related macular degeneration, verteporfin photodynamic therapy reduced the risk of moderate or severe vision loss compared with sham treatment over two years.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the TAP randomized placebo-controlled trial and related TAP reports, which state that verteporfin reduced the risk of losing at least 15 letters in patients with subfoveal CNV from AMD, with benefit evident through 12 months and referenced as sustained over 24 months; TAP report No. 4 explicitly notes that in this investigation verteporfin reduced the risk of at least moderate vision loss, while presenting 24-month superiority on contrast sensitivity outcomes (PMID: 12441717; PMID: 10532441). Several additional articles are indirect or lower-quality support: reviews/commentaries state that photodynamic therapy may reduce severe visual acuity loss (PMID: 11917793), observational or non-sham-controlled studies report less visual loss with verteporfin at 12 months or versus historical/untreated controls (PMID: 12608309; PMID: 15197060), and health-economic/quality-of-life analyses assume or derive benefit from randomized trial data versus placebo (PMID: 11714119; PMID: 15513444). A recurring theme is that benefit is strongest in predominantly classic subfoveal CNV, whereas benefit is less clear in minimally classic lesions (PMID: 10532441; PMID: 12441717). Some evidence items are only partial matches to the claim because they address 1-year outcomes, nonrandomized comparisons, or secondary outcomes rather than the exact 2-year visual-loss endpoint, creating a scope discrepancy rather than true conflict.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The evidence includes a pivotal randomized placebo-controlled TAP trial and related 24-month TAP reporting indicating verteporfin reduced clinically meaningful vision loss risk in subfoveal AMD-related CNV, although several supporting items are indirect or do not exactly match the 2-year sham-controlled endpoint.

support_highlights.snippet: In the Treatment of Age-Related Macular Degeneration With Photodynamic Therapy (TAP) investigation, verteporfin therapy reduced the risk of at least moderate vision loss (defined as a loss of at least 15 letters of visual acuity) in patients with subfoveal choroidal neovascularization (CNV) secondary to age-related macular degeneration (ARMD).

OBJECTIVE: To determine if photodynamic therapy with verteporfin (Visudyne; CIBA Vision Corp, Duluth, Ga) can safely reduce the risk of vision loss in patients with subfoveal choroidal neovascularization (CNV) caused by age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

At the month-12 examination, 246 (61%) of 402 eyes assigned to verteporfin compared with 96 (46%) of 207 eyes assigned to placebo had lost fewer than 15 letters of visual acuity from baseline (P<.001).

CONCLUSIONS: Since verteporfin therapy of subfoveal CNV from AMD can safely reduce the risk of vision loss, we recommend verteporfin therapy for treatment of patients with predominantly classic CNV from AMD.

The Treatment of Age-Related Macular Degeneration with Photodynamic Therapy (TAP) Study showed that at 1 year, photodynamic therapy significantly reduced the chances of severe visual loss (15 letters or greater) compared with placebo treatment in patients with "predominantly classic" subfoveal choroidal neovascularization (CNV).
Laser photocoagulation and photodynamic therapy may reduce the risk of severe loss of visual acuity in subfoveal CNV secondary to age-related macular degeneration.

Photodynamic therapy with the preparation Visudyne (PDT) is the only treatment which retards statistically significantly the decline of vision in patients with age related and myopic macular degeneration with a subfoveal, predominantly classic choroidal neovascularization.
RESULTS: The lost of visual acuity was significantly reduced in the verteporfin--treated eyes compared--with controls.
At the 12 month 73.91% eyes of PDT group versus 36.84% of control group (p < 0.001) lost fewer than 3 Snellen lines.

CONCLUSIONS: Ophthalmologists should consider interpreting fluorescein angiographic images of subfoveal lesions with terms provided to follow recommendations regarding which patients are most likely to benefit from verteporfin therapy based on results from the TAP Investigation and VIP Trial.
Patients who received PDT were 2.9 times (95% confidence interval, 0.9-9.1) less likely to develop a moderate (>2 lines) visual loss (chi(2) = 3.2, P =.07).
Effectiveness for verteporfin therapy and for placebo was calculated in terms of vision years: 1.068 and 0.494, respectively.
The study demonstrated that greater effectiveness of verteporfin therapy versus placebo compensated for the cost of the therapy so that verteporfin therapy was clearly costeffective.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and occult-with-no-classic choroidal neovascularization, verteporfin photodynamic therapy produced better visual outcomes than sham treatment, including less loss of visual acuity and less progression of neovascularization.
support_summary: Support is indirect rather than definitive. Exploratory analyses of TAP/VIP data found that in occult-with-no-classic lesions, treatment benefit with verteporfin was greater for smaller lesions, and smaller occult lesions had visual acuity outcomes similar to predominantly classic lesions, suggesting some visual benefit in this subgroup (PMID: 12967792). An open-label occult-only study reported that 82.4% of eyes lost fewer than 30 letters, 61.8% lost fewer than 15 letters, and fluorescein leakage from CNV decreased markedly over 12 months, supporting less visual loss and reduced leakage/progression with verteporfin, but without a sham comparator (PMID: 18583995). A retrospective New Zealand outcomes study also found many AMD patients avoided moderate visual loss after PDT and stated results were consistent with prior placebo-controlled trials, but it did not isolate occult-with-no-classic lesions or directly compare against sham in its own data (PMID: 17300567). Overall, the supporting set mainly shows favorable verteporfin outcomes and anatomical improvement, with a discrepancy that none of these abstracts directly demonstrates superiority over sham in the target subgroup.
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is only weakly relevant to the specific claim. One prospective comparative study found final visual acuity worsened after PDT and that bevacizumab had better efficacy than PDT in neovascular AMD, but it did not compare PDT with sham and was not specific to occult-with-no-classic lesions as claimed (PMID: 22648253). Another analysis from ranibizumab trials concluded ranibizumab outperformed sham or PDT for driving-related outcomes and vision, but again this does not directly test whether PDT was better than sham in occult-with-no-classic CNV (PMID: 23009891). Thus, these studies challenge the relative effectiveness of PDT versus newer therapy or show decline under PDT, yet they do not directly contradict the specific sham-controlled statement.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, but only modestly by the provided evidence. The supporting studies show verteporfin is associated with less visual loss and reduced CNV leakage in occult-with-no-classic AMD, whereas the contradicting studies do not directly address the sham-controlled comparison in the target subgroup.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and occult-with-no-classic choroidal neovascularization, the benefit of verteporfin photodynamic therapy was greater in eyes with smaller lesions or poorer baseline visual acuity than in eyes with larger lesions or better baseline visual acuity.
support_summary: The strongest and most directly relevant evidence comes from pooled exploratory analyses of the TAP/VIP randomized trial datasets, which specifically found in occult-with-no-classic lesions a significant treatment-by-lesion-size interaction, with smaller verteporfin-treated lesions losing less vision than larger lesions; however, treatment interaction with baseline visual acuity was not significant, so this study supports the lesion-size part of the claim but not the poorer-baseline-VA part (PMID: 12967792). Several retrospective/clinical series also report that smaller baseline CNV size predicted better verteporfin PDT outcomes, including in occult CNV subgroups, or that increasing lesion size predicted greater visual loss after PDT (PMID: 18301052, PMID: 17539784, PMID: 15606458, PMID: 16225928). Evidence regarding baseline visual acuity is discrepant with the claim: multiple studies reported that better, not poorer, baseline visual acuity predicted better final visual outcome after PDT (PMID: 14962414, PMID: 16187598, PMID: 18301052, PMID: 18949910), meaning some items in the support set are only partially relevant or actually conflict with the baseline-VA portion. Overall, the literature in this set consistently favors greater benefit with smaller lesions, but does not consistently support greater benefit with poorer baseline visual acuity in occult-with-no-classic CNV.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. Nonetheless, within the studies listed as supportive, several abstracts actually conflict with the claim's poorer-baseline-visual-acuity component by stating that better baseline visual acuity predicted better visual outcomes after PDT (PMID: 14962414, PMID: 16187598, PMID: 18301052, PMID: 18949910).
conclusion: The statement is overall only partially supported. Evidence is fairly strong that verteporfin PDT benefit was greater in smaller lesions, including in occult-with-no-classic CNV, but the claim that benefit was greater in eyes with poorer baseline visual acuity is not supported by the strongest direct trial analysis and is contradicted by several clinical series that found better baseline acuity predicted better outcomes.

support_highlights.snippet: In the multiple linear regression model of individual lesion compositions, there was a significant treatment-by-lesion-size interaction for minimally classic and occult with no classic lesions, but not for predominantly classic lesions.
Interaction between treatment and baseline visual acuity was not significant for any lesion composition.
Small verteporfin-treated lesions lost less vision than large verteporfin-treated lesions in each lesion composition.

In the multiple linear regression model that included all lesion compositions, lesion size was a more significant predictive factor for the magnitude of treatment benefit than either lesion composition or visual acuity.

Using multivariate logistic regression, loss of logMAR vision at 12 months was independently associated with increasing age (P=0.004), better baseline vision (P=0.009) and increasing lesion size (P=0.035).

In groups II and IV, smaller baseline CNV sizes also favorably influenced final BCVA.
Irrespective of the CNV type, better BCVA at presentation represents a good predictive sign.
A visual acuity of 0.4 or more 1 year after PDT was predicted by an acuity of 0.4 or more (p = 0.01) and occult with no classic CNV (p = 0.04) prior to PDT.
Factors predicting a visual acuity of 0.4 or more 1 year after PDT were an acuity of 0.4 or more and occult with no classic CNV prior to PDT.

claim: The Age-Related Eye Disease Study fundus-photograph grading system showed moderate to substantial agreement for grading a range of age-related macular degeneration abnormalities, including pigment epithelial detachment, retinal hemorrhage or exudates, geographic atrophy, pigment changes, and drusen characteristics.
support_summary: One supporting study found moderate agreement when grading age-related maculopathy features across different fundus photography techniques, with exact agreement of 72% to 89% for drusen, retinal pigment epithelial degeneration, increased pigment, geographic atrophy, and exudative macular degeneration, and kappa values ranging from 0.33 to 0.60 (PMID: 1454343). These findings are broadly consistent with the claim's emphasis on moderate to substantial agreement for several AMD-related abnormalities. However, this study did not specifically evaluate the Age-Related Eye Disease Study fundus-photograph grading system itself, and it addressed comparison of imaging techniques rather than reproducibility of that exact grading system, so its support is indirect and only partial.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is weakly to moderately supported overall because the available evidence shows comparable moderate agreement for grading several AMD-related lesions, but it does not directly test the AREDS fundus-photograph grading system named in the claim. With no contradictory evidence present, the main limitation is indirectness rather than inconsistency.

support_highlights.snippet: Corresponding photographic fields were graded by a masked grader for the presence of any drusen, soft drusen, retinal pigment epithelial degeneration, increased retinal pigmentation, and early and late age-related maculopathy.

Exact agreement between gradings of the 45 degrees photographs taken through nonpharmacologically dilated pupils and 30 degrees photographs taken through dilated pupils was 75% for any drusen, 72% for soft drusen, 72% for retinal pigment epithelial degeneration, 74% for increased retinal pigment, 85% for pure geographic atrophy, and 89% for exudative macular degeneration.
The kappa scores varied from 0.33 for geographic atrophy to 0.60 for exudative macular degeneration.

claim: The Age-Related Eye Disease Study classification system for age-related macular degeneration was suitable for longitudinal multicenter studies because grading showed little temporal drift over repeated evaluations.
support_summary: One AREDS2 methods/reproducibility study directly supports the claim that the AREDS grading/classification approach was stable over time and suitable for longitudinal multicenter use. The abstract reports formal temporal-drift testing by annually regrading a subset of baseline images and found 91% of images were graded within two steps of baseline on the AREDS severity scale, with substantial agreement (weighted kappa 0.73), and historical AREDS film-based data showed similarly strong temporal stability (88% within two steps; PMID: 23620429). It also states there was no difference in AREDS2-AREDS concordance for temporal drift, reinforcing continuity with the original AREDS system and supporting its longitudinal applicability across study settings (PMID: 23620429).
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. Although the evidence here comes from a single reproducibility paper, it directly evaluates temporal drift and reports stable repeated grading with concordance comparable to historical AREDS, which fits the claim well.

support_highlights.snippet: The reproducibility of digital grading of AREDS2 images was assessed by reproducibility exercises, temporal drift (regrading a subset of baseline annually, n = 88), and contemporaneous masked regrading (ongoing, monthly regrade on 5% of submissions, n = 1335 eyes).

In AREDS2, 91% and 96% of images received replicate grades within two steps of the baseline value on the AREDS severity scale for temporal drift and contemporaneous assessment, respectively (weighted Kappa of 0.73 and 0.76).
Historical data for temporal drift in replicate gradings on the AREDS film-based images were 88% within two steps (weighted Kappa = 0.88).
There was no difference in AREDS2-AREDS concordance for temporal drift (exact P = 0.57).
Digital color grading has nearly the same reproducibility as historical film grading.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization who are not eligible for laser or photodynamic therapy, subretinal membrane excision with simultaneous autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation improves visual acuity more often than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: Most supporting studies are uncontrolled case series showing that autologous retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) transplantation performed with choroidal neovascular membrane excision can improve postoperative visual acuity in AMD, including 57% of eyes improving by at least 2 lines in a pilot study of foveal CNV (PMID: 11812425), improved ETDRS scores after autologous RPE-Bruch's membrane complex transplantation in hemorrhagic AMD (PMID: 19117919), and significant postoperative visual acuity improvement in a small retrospective series using an RPE/choroid patch (PMID: 31454718). The strongest direct support comes from a retrospective comparison in neovascular/hemorrhagic AMD, where visual acuity improvement of at least 2 lines was significantly more frequent with RPE transplantation (62.5% and 58.0% in two graft subgroups) than with CNV excision alone (23.5%) (PMID: 27496526). One additional study of autologous iris pigment epithelium, not retinal pigment epithelium, reported improvement or stable vision after membrane removal plus transplantation, but its relevance to the specific claim is indirect and it lacks a comparison with excision alone (PMID: 11030816). Overall, the supportive literature trends toward visual benefit with adjunctive RPE-based transplantation, but much of it is limited by small samples, nonrandomized designs, heterogeneous graft types, and frequent absence of a direct excision-alone comparator.
contradict_summary: The main contradicting evidence is a nonrandomized comparison of 7 AMD patients receiving autologous iris pigment epithelial cell transplantation after membrane removal versus 15 controls undergoing membrane removal alone, which found no significant improvement in macular function compared with controls (PMID: 10896035). However, this study used iris pigment epithelium rather than retinal pigment epithelium, so it is not a perfect match to the claim and partly reflects a tissue-type discrepancy. Thus, it weakens the idea that pigment epithelial transplantation necessarily outperforms excision alone, but it is less directly contradictory to the specific RPE-transplant claim than it first appears.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The best direct comparative evidence shows RPE transplantation with membrane excision yields visual improvement more often than excision alone (PMID: 27496526), while the main negative study used iris pigment epithelium rather than RPE and therefore only partially challenges the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: Five patients showed improved visual acuity of 3 to 4 lines, 13 patients had stable visual acuity (+/-2 lines), and 2 patients had reduced visual acuity of 6 lines.
In this pilot study, the transplantation of autologous IPE cells was done as an addition to conventional surgical excision of choroidal neovascular membranes.

Transplanted cells were well tolerated in the subretinal space and did not adversely affect the function of the photoreceptors, since improvement or stable visual acuity was observed in 18 patients after IPE transplantation.

Postoperatively, after median observation of 17 months (range, 12 to 24 months) best-corrected visual acuity was improved 2 or more lines in eight eyes (57.1%), remained the same (+/- 1 line) in five eyes (35%), and decreased by more than 2 lines in one eye (7.1%).

Conclusions: In eyes with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization, autotransplantation of retinal pigment epithelium was performed in addition to conventional removal of the choroidal neovascularization without significant intraoperative or postoperative complications.
Visual acuity improvement of 2 or more lines in 57% of the eyes was achieved.
This procedure consisted of excision of the choroidal neovascular membrane and transplantation of autologous retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)-Bruch's membrane complex.
On the last follow-up visit, the mean for the ETDRS scores increased from 28.65 +/- 23.99 before surgery to 47.76 +/- 17.22 after surgery.
The transplantation of the autologous RPE-Bruch's membrane complex can increase the visual acuity of patients with hemorrhagic AMD.

The percentage of visual acuity (VA) improvement (at least two or more lines changed) in subgroup 1 is 62.5%, which is significantly higher than that in CNV excision group (23.5%), p = 0.02.
The percentage of VA improvement in subgroup 2 is 58.0%, which is also significantly higher than that in CNV excision group (23.5%), p = 0.02.
The autologous RPE transplantation can increase the vision of patients with haemorrhagic AMD.

After removal of the CNVM, the autologous full-thickness patch of the RPE, bruch's membrane, choriocapillaris, and choroid was excised from the midperiphery and placed under the macula.
There was a statistically significant increase in visual acuity at the postoperative final visit compared to baseline. (p = 0.027)

In conclusion, the treatment resulted in no significant improvement in macular function, as compared with the control; however, no rejection or deterioration in visual acuity occurred up to the 13 month follow up.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization, subretinal membrane excision with simultaneous autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation produces better reading acuity and higher multifocal electroretinography response density than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: One retrospective study reported that, among patients with neovascular AMD undergoing CNV membrane excision, adding autologous RPE transplantation was associated with significantly greater improvement in ETDRS visual acuity and a higher proportion achieving at least two-line VA improvement than membrane excision alone (PMID: 27496526). The abstract also notes restoration of central fixation stability in some transplanted eyes, suggesting functional benefit beyond standard excision (PMID: 27496526). However, this study involved haemorrhagic neovascular AMD rather than specifically foveal CNV, and it evaluated general visual acuity/fixation rather than the claim’s specific outcomes of reading acuity and multifocal electroretinography response density, so support is indirect. No discrepancy was found within this item, but its relevance to the exact claim is only partial.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall. The available study suggests autologous RPE transplantation can improve visual outcomes versus membrane excision alone, but it does not directly assess the specific endpoints of reading acuity or multifocal electroretinography response density described in the claim.

The percentage of visual acuity (VA) improvement (at least two or more lines changed) in subgroup 1 is 62.5%, which is significantly higher than that in CNV excision group (23.5%), p = 0.02.
In subgroup 2, VAS increased from 16.61 ± 27.98 to 29.16 ± 23.80 (p = 0.02) in a mean period of 21.72 ± 11.09 months.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and foveal choroidal neovascularization, autologous retinal pigment epithelial cell transplantation appears to be a beneficial adjunct to membrane excision rather than membrane excision alone.
support_summary: Several case series and a retrospective comparison suggest that adding autologous RPE transplantation to CNV membrane excision can improve visual outcomes in neovascular/hemorrhagic AMD. A pilot study reported that after combined subretinal surgery plus autologous RPE transplantation, 57.1% of eyes improved by at least 2 lines, with no CNV recurrence during follow-up, supporting benefit of the adjunctive approach (PMID: 11812425). The strongest comparative evidence comes from a retrospective cohort in which both RPE-Bruch's membrane and RPE monolayer graft groups had significantly higher rates of visual improvement than CNV excision alone (62.5% and 58.0% vs 23.5%) (PMID: 27496526). Additional non-comparative studies of RPE/choroid patch grafts found feasibility, viable grafts, improved fixation or vision in some patients, and surviving functional grafts, though they often stressed need for longer follow-up and noted complications (PMID: 14516809, PMID: 17324698, PMID: 19117919). Two cited SUPPORT items are only indirectly relevant because they studied iris pigment epithelium rather than retinal pigment epithelium, so they should be treated as mechanistically suggestive rather than direct support (PMID: 11030816, PMID: 12239351).
contradict_summary: One controlled but nonrandomized study found that autologous iris pigment epithelial cell transplantation after neovascular membrane removal produced no significant improvement in macular function compared with membrane excision alone, although no rejection or visual deterioration was seen (PMID: 10896035). This contradicting evidence is only partially aligned with the claim because it used iris pigment epithelium rather than retinal pigment epithelium, limiting direct applicability. It nonetheless raises the theme that pigment epithelial adjunctive transplantation may not always outperform excision alone in AMD.
conclusion: Overall, the claim is more supported than contradicted. The best available directly relevant evidence includes one comparative study favoring RPE transplantation plus membrane excision over excision alone and multiple supportive case series, whereas the main contradiction comes from an indirectly related iris pigment epithelium study rather than autologous RPE transplantation itself.

support_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: Postoperatively, after median observation of 17 months (range, 12 to 24 months) best-corrected visual acuity was improved 2 or more lines in eight eyes (57.1%), remained the same (+/- 1 line) in five eyes (35%), and decreased by more than 2 lines in one eye (7.1%).
Pairwise t test showed significant improvement after 1 month (P = .0031, P = .0062) as well as 1 year (P = .0066, P = .0105).

The percentage of VA improvement in subgroup 2 is 58.0%, which is also significantly higher than that in CNV excision group (23.5%), p = 0.02.
CONCLUSIONS: The autologous RPE transplantation can increase the vision of patients with haemorrhagic AMD.

Surgical excision of choroidal neovascularizations (CNV) is technically feasible but invariably associated with inadvertent removal of corresponding retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and subsequent atrophy of the choriocapillaris, with the latter two layers being a prerequisite for normal photoreceptor function.
Therefore, techniques for translocating intact autologous RPE cell sheets have been sought and preservation of foveal neurosensory functions has recently been successfully demonstrated.

PURPOSE: To assess excision of choroidal new vessels (CNV) combined with autologous transplantation of the equatorial retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) as a means of restoring vision for patients with acute neovascular age-related macular degeneration (AMD).
Successful viable grafts were seen in 11 patients.
Three patients had good visual function on the grafts, with mean logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (logMAR) improving from 0.88 to 0.79 and maintained beyond 1 year.
CONCLUSIONS: Autologous RPE transplantation can in principle restore vision in neovascular AMD, but surgical complications remain high.

On the last follow-up visit, the mean for the ETDRS scores increased from 28.65 +/- 23.99 before surgery to 47.76 +/- 17.22 after surgery.
CONCLUSIONS: The transplantation of the autologous RPE-Bruch's membrane complex can increase the visual acuity of patients with hemorrhagic AMD.
The surviving transplanted graft with functional overlying retina was observed after surgery.

In conclusion, the treatment resulted in no significant improvement in macular function, as compared with the control; however, no rejection or deterioration in visual acuity occurred up to the 13 month follow up.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration treated with intravitreal ranibizumab, an optical coherence tomography-guided variable-dosing regimen improved visual acuity and reduced central retinal thickness.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the PrONTO OCT-guided variable-dosing study, which reported at 24 months a mean visual acuity gain of 11.1 letters and a 212 μm reduction in OCT central retinal thickness with relatively few injections, closely matching the claim (PMID: 19376495). Additional studies of OCT-guided, PRN, or flexible ranibizumab regimens generally also found improved visual acuity and reduced central/foveal/macular thickness, including high-frequency SD-OCT follow-up (PMID: 33767261), OCT-based retreatment (PMID: 19730802), PRN treatment with monthly monitoring (PMID: 21400056, PMID: 24999724), and flexible regimens with significant foveal thickness reduction (PMID: 21499194). Some support is indirect because several studies emphasize visual or thickness benefit without clearly matching the exact PrONTO-style OCT-guided variable-dosing regimen, and some focus only on one outcome or special subgroups such as large submacular hemorrhage or extrafoveal CNV (PMID: 23851632, PMID: 27069359, PMID: 29445522). One supposedly supporting item mainly documents improved visual acuity and reduced macular thickness after OCT-guided retreatment while focusing on retinal safety rather than efficacy, so it is supportive but secondary to the claim (PMID: 22888205). Overall, the support side shows a recurring theme that ranibizumab retreatment guided by OCT/activity can improve visual outcomes and reduce retinal thickness, though several studies are uncontrolled or observational.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is mixed and often only partially relevant. One short-term study in exudative disciform scars found significant reductions in OCT central foveal thickness and macular volume but no significant change in visual acuity, contradicting the visual-acuity portion of the claim in a scarred, chronic subgroup rather than typical treatment-naive neovascular AMD (PMID: 19634740). A retrospective comparison of ranibizumab schedules reported no significant difference in mean visual acuity or central macular thickness between groups, but also stated that standard OCT-guided retreatment protocols achieved smaller mean vision gains than monthly dosing and that loading-dose treatment produced more ≥15-letter gains than baseline PRN treatment, suggesting OCT-guided variable dosing may be less effective than fixed monthly dosing for vision gains (PMID: 20693484). Longer-term follow-up of an OCT- and VA-guided variable-dosing regimen found worsened mean BCVA and increased mean central retinal thickness over 5 years, which directly conflicts with sustained improvement, though the authors attribute this to undertreatment rather than failure of ranibizumab itself (PMID: 27354758). support_summary: The strongest supporting evidence is the 2-year PrONTO study, which explicitly reports that OCT-guided variable ranibizumab dosing produced significant visual acuity improvement and central retinal thickness reduction, with outcomes stated to be comparable to phase III trials while using only 9.9 injections over 24 months (PMID: 19376495). A retrospective OCT-based ranibizumab series also found good visual outcomes with a mean of 6 injections over about 16 months and concluded that OCT-guided care reduced injections versus monthly dosing, although it did not directly compare outcomes with phase III studies or emphasize anatomic results (PMID: 19730802). Another retrospective study of individualized ranibizumab/bevacizumab treatment suggests fewer ranibizumab injections may be possible, but it is only indirectly relevant because it is not specifically an OCT-guided ranibizumab-only comparison to phase III outcomes and does not clearly report anatomic equivalence (PMID: 22560423). contradict_summary: A Canadian retrospective practice review found that ranibizumab-treated neovascular AMD patients received a mean of 5.1 injections in 12 months but had visual outcomes that compared poorly with randomized clinical trials, directly challenging the idea that fewer-injection real-world regimens maintain phase III-like results (PMID: 21135894). Another retrospective comparison reported that standard OCT-guided retreatment protocols achieved smaller mean vision gains than monthly ranibizumab, and that a loading-dose approach produced more visual gains than PRN treatment, which contradicts the claim of similar outcomes under variable dosing (PMID: 20693484). These studies mainly question whether reduced-injection or PRN/OCT-guided use reproduces phase III visual benefits in routine practice. Neither contradicting abstract squarely refutes anatomic similarity in the exact PrONTO regimen, but both weaken the generalizability of the claim.
conclusion: The claim is overall supported, but with important reservations. The direct PrONTO evidence strongly matches the claim (PMID: 19376495), yet multiple retrospective real-world studies suggest that lower-frequency or PRN/OCT-guided ranibizumab regimens may yield worse visual outcomes than clinical trials, so the claim appears most valid for the specific PrONTO setting rather than universally.

support_summary: Most supporting studies describe ranibizumab pro re nata or variable-dosing regimens in neovascular AMD in which OCT findings directly triggered retreatment, commonly based on recurrent/persistent macular fluid or increased central retinal thickness, and reported favorable visual/anatomic outcomes with fewer injections than fixed monthly dosing (PMID: 19376495, PMID: 19730802, PMID: 22733165). The strongest direct evidence is the PrONTO study, where monthly retreatment criteria explicitly included OCT-measured CRT increase and later any qualitative increase in fluid on OCT, showing good 24-month outcomes with relatively few injections (PMID: 19376495). Additional studies and case series similarly state that reinjection decisions were based on OCT findings, sometimes alongside vision, angiography, or clinical examination, supporting OCT as a practical tool for retreatment timing rather than as the sole determinant (PMID: 19815292, PMID: 20539256, PMID: 22560423, PMID: 23851632, PMID: 24999724). One study found very high interobserver agreement for retreatment decisions using spectral-domain OCT (k=0.88), reinforcing reproducibility of OCT-based activity assessment after ranibizumab loading (PMID: 29232336). Some included items are indirect or secondary evidence, such as a review citing PrONTO or a biomarker study linking baseline OCT features to later injection frequency rather than directly testing retreatment decisions, so the support base is heterogeneous (PMID: 19668384, PMID: 39287532).
contradict_summary: There is only one contradicting item, and it does not directly show that OCT was not useful for deciding retreatment. Instead, it reports that in German routine care there were few ophthalmic reviews including OCT, low injection frequency, and suboptimal outcomes, which suggests underuse of monitoring rather than failure of OCT as a retreatment tool (PMID: 24838864). This makes the item more of an indirect health-services critique than true contradiction of the claim. Therefore, the contradicting evidence is weak and somewhat miscategorized relative to the specific statement.
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. support_summary: A pivotal sham-controlled phase III trial directly supports the claim: monthly ranibizumab in neovascular AMD with minimally classic or occult choroidal neovascularization led to markedly better visual acuity outcomes than sham, including higher proportions losing fewer than 15 letters, more patients gaining at least 15 letters, and mean letter gains versus mean loss with sham (PMID: 17021318). claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration–related subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, ranibizumab was well tolerated and had a low incidence of serious ocular or nonocular adverse events compared with sham injection.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the sham-controlled MARINA trial, where ranibizumab for minimally classic or occult subfoveal CNV secondary to AMD was reported to have "low rates of serious adverse events," with presumed endophthalmitis in 1.0% and serious uveitis in 1.3% over 24 months, while demonstrating major efficacy versus sham injection (PMID: 17021318). Additional ranibizumab studies in neovascular/subfoveal CNV populations consistently describe the drug as well tolerated with low rates of serious ocular and nonocular adverse events, including ANCHOR and its 2-year follow-up, though these compare against verteporfin/PDT rather than sham and therefore only indirectly support the specific comparator in the claim (PMID: 17021319, PMID: 19118696). Open-label and large safety studies likewise report that ranibizumab was safe/well tolerated and that serious ocular events were uncommon, but these lack sham controls and are less directly relevant to the exact statement (PMID: 16581423, PMID: 19643495, PMID: 20163368, PMID: 31764612, PMID: 38384637). Reviews also summarize phase III trial evidence as showing ranibizumab to be safe and well tolerated, but these are secondary sources and not direct sham-controlled evidence (PMID: 21221192, PMID: 22911433). Overall, the support side is dominated by one highly relevant sham-controlled trial plus multiple concordant but indirect safety reports.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly indirect to the exact claim. The main challenge comes from the FOCUS trial, where ranibizumab combined with verteporfin PDT was associated with notable serious ocular inflammation (11.4%) and endophthalmitis/presumed endophthalmitis, indicating that ranibizumab-containing treatment was not uniformly low-risk in all settings; however, this was combination therapy rather than ranibizumab versus sham alone, so applicability is imperfect (PMID: 17101999). A retrospective series reported six macular hemorrhages after anti-VEGF injections, all after ranibizumab in previously treated eyes with occult CNV or large pigment epithelial detachment, but this was not a controlled comparison with sham and may reflect a selected high-risk population (PMID: 19769866). One case report of radiation maculopathy occurred after epimacular brachytherapy given concomitantly with ranibizumab, but the adverse event is plausibly attributable to radiation rather than ranibizumab itself, making this a weak contradiction and a discrepancy in categorization (PMID: 25390433). Thus, the contradicting literature mainly raises safety concerns in combination-therapy or special-risk contexts rather than directly refuting the sham-controlled claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. A directly relevant sham-controlled trial supports that ranibizumab was well tolerated with low rates of serious adverse events versus sham injection, while the opposing evidence is mostly indirect, involving combination therapy, retrospective high-risk cases, or a case report not clearly attributable to ranibizumab alone.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, intravitreal ranibizumab provided better visual acuity outcomes than verteporfin photodynamic therapy, with more patients maintaining or improving vision and fewer patients losing vision.
support_summary: The strongest evidence directly supports the claim: the ANCHOR randomized trial in predominantly classic neovascular AMD found ranibizumab markedly outperformed verteporfin PDT at 12 months, with 94.3% to 96.4% of ranibizumab-treated patients losing fewer than 15 letters versus 64.3% with verteporfin, 35.7% to 40.3% gaining at least 15 letters versus 5.6%, and mean visual acuity improving rather than declining (PMID: 17021319). Additional evidence from a trial of ranibizumab plus PDT versus PDT alone also showed better vision preservation with ranibizumab-containing therapy, though this is not a pure head-to-head monotherapy comparison and is therefore indirect for the claim (PMID: 17101999). A review article summarized that over 90% of ranibizumab-treated patients versus about 68% of verteporfin-treated patients had no tangible vision loss, again supporting superior visual outcomes with ranibizumab, though this is secondary evidence (PMID: 18354841). Longer-term functional outcomes from ANCHOR/MARINA analyses showed ranibizumab-treated patients were more likely than PDT-treated patients to still be driving and to achieve 20/40 or better vision, which is supportive but less directly tied to the exact claim wording (PMID: 23009891). Small exploratory studies comparing ranibizumab monotherapy with ranibizumab+PDT found no added visual benefit from verteporfin and in one pilot study better letter gains with ranibizumab alone, but these are indirect and not direct ranibizumab-versus-PDT monotherapy comparisons in the exact target population (PMID: 20577273, PMID: 21124254, PMID: 21872935).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This is driven primarily by a large direct randomized comparison in predominantly classic subfoveal neovascular AMD showing ranibizumab produced substantially better visual acuity outcomes than verteporfin PDT, with more patients maintaining or improving vision and fewer losing vision (PMID: 17021319), while the remaining studies are mostly consistent but more indirect.

Mean visual acuity increased by 8.5 letters in the 0.3-mg group and 11.3 letters in the 0.5-mg group, as compared with a decrease of 9.5 letters in the verteporfin group (P<0.001 for each comparison).
CONCLUSIONS: Ranibizumab was superior to verteporfin as intravitreal treatment of predominantly classic neovascular age-related macular degeneration, with low rates of serious ocular adverse events.
RESULTS: At 12 months, 90.5% of the ranibizumab-treated patients and 67.9% of the control patients had lost fewer than 15 letters (P<.001).
CONCLUSION/APPLICATION TO CLINICAL PRACTICE: Ranibizumab + PDT was more efficacious than PDT alone for treating neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

(3) Clinical evaluation of ranibizumab includes 2 placebo-controlled trials (900 patients in total), a trial versus verteporfin (423 patients), and a trial testing ranibizumab in combination with verteporfin (162 patients).

More than 90% of patients treated with ranibizumab in these two trials had no tangible loss of vision for one to two years, compared to about 68% of patients treated with verteporfin (statistically significant difference).

(6) When visual acuity continues to deteriorate in patients with age-related macular degeneration despite treatment with verteporfin, ranibizumab provides an effective alternative for patients with no particular risk factors for stroke.
Among patients driving at baseline in the ANCHOR trial at 2 years after randomization, 71.6% (95% CI, 60.8-82.4) of PDT patients and 91.4% (95% CI, 85.3-97.5) of 0.5-mg patients were still driving.

CONCLUSIONS: These results suggest that patients with neovascular AMD treated with ranibizumab are more likely to report driving ability and have vision of at least 20/40 than patients given sham treatment or PDT.
RESULTS: In all, 18 patients were recruited. The PDT group gained a mean of 2.2 ETDRS letters at 1 year and the sham group gained a mean of 4.4 letters (P=0.47).

CONCLUSION: The addition of standard-fluence verteporfin PDT at baseline to a ranibizumab regimen conferred no benefit in terms of visual acuity or number of ranibizumab injections required at 1 year.
RESULTS: Except for 1 eye in combination therapy, all eyes in both groups lost <15 letters of visual acuity.
At 12 months, there was a mean gain of +12 letters and +3.2 letters for monotherapy and combination therapy, respectively (relative percent change of 32% vs. 7%, P = 0.03).
CONCLUSION: Ranibizumab monotherapy showed greater improvement in visual acuity versus combination therapy.
In patients who developed RPE tears, better visual acuity (VA) outcomes were observed in those treated with ranibizumab versus control treatment.

Mean VA was better in patients who developed RPE tears while receiving ranibizumab than in those who received control treatment, suggesting a potential benefit of continued ranibizumab therapy in patients with neovascular AMD who developed RPE tears.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, intravitreal ranibizumab produced more favorable retinal lesion anatomic changes than verteporfin photodynamic therapy.
support_summary: Two FOCUS trial reports suggest that adding intravitreal ranibizumab to verteporfin photodynamic therapy (PDT) improved outcomes versus PDT alone in predominantly classic neovascular AMD. The stronger anatomic evidence comes from the 2-year report, which states that ranibizumab+PDT showed less lesion growth and greater reduction of CNV leakage and subretinal fluid accumulation than PDT alone, directly supporting more favorable lesion-related anatomic changes (PMID: 18321465). The 1-year report mainly supports superior efficacy by visual acuity preservation but does not clearly report retinal lesion anatomic changes in the abstract, so it is only indirect/partial support for the specific claim (PMID: 17101999). A notable discrepancy is that both studies evaluate ranibizumab combined with PDT versus PDT alone, rather than ranibizumab monotherapy versus PDT, so applicability to the exact claim is somewhat indirect.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because the available evidence includes one abstract with direct lesion-anatomy advantages for ranibizumab-containing therapy over PDT alone and another with indirect efficacy support. However, support is moderate rather than strong because the cited studies tested ranibizumab plus PDT, not ranibizumab alone, and only one abstract directly describes anatomic lesion changes.

support_highlights.snippet: On average, ranibizumab + PDT patients exhibited less lesion growth and greater reduction of CNV leakage and subretinal fluid accumulation, and required fewer PDT retreatments, than PDT-alone patients (mean = 0.4 vs 3.0 PDT retreatments).
CONCLUSION/APPLICATION TO CLINICAL PRACTICE: Ranibizumab + PDT was more efficacious than PDT alone for treating neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration and new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal choroidal neovascularization, ranibizumab and verteporfin photodynamic therapy had similarly low rates of serious ocular and nonocular adverse events.
support_summary: Several small prospective/open-label studies of combination verteporfin PDT plus ranibizumab reported few or no ocular/systemic adverse events, suggesting an overall low serious-event burden, but these studies do not directly compare ranibizumab alone with verteporfin alone as in the claim (PMID: 18667951, PMID: 18772178, PMID: 18780651, PMID: 19878113). A larger randomized trial found that safety and tolerability were similar across ranibizumab monotherapy and verteporfin-combination regimens, with low numbers of ocular serious adverse events occurring mostly as single cases, which is the most directly relevant support for similar safety rates (PMID: 22444829). Across the support set, the dominant theme is low observed serious ocular and nonocular adverse-event rates with ranibizumab-containing regimens. However, much of this evidence is indirect because it evaluates combination therapy rather than the head-to-head ranibizumab-versus-verteporfin comparison in new-onset predominantly classic subfoveal CNV.
contradict_summary: The strongest and most directly relevant evidence is the head-to-head ANCHOR trial, which compared ranibizumab with verteporfin in predominantly classic neovascular AMD; its abstract reports low serious ocular adverse-event rates for ranibizumab but does not provide verteporfin serious adverse-event rates, so the contradiction label appears overstated based on the abstract alone (PMID: 17021319). Two FOCUS trial reports compared ranibizumab plus PDT with PDT alone and found higher rates of serious ocular inflammation/endophthalmitis in the ranibizumab-containing arm, while serious nonocular adverse events were similar between groups, contradicting the idea of similarly low serious ocular event rates, though these studies address combination therapy rather than ranibizumab monotherapy (PMID: 17101999, PMID: 18321465). The main recurring theme in the contradicting set is excess serious ocular inflammatory events in ranibizumab-containing combination regimens versus PDT alone. Overall, this set includes relevant safety concerns but is partly indirect and partly mismatched to the exact claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat more contradicted than supported. Although multiple studies suggest low serious adverse-event rates with ranibizumab-containing regimens and one larger trial reports similar safety across regimens, the more directly comparative evidence raises concern that ranibizumab-containing treatment can have higher serious ocular inflammatory event rates than PDT-based control therapy, while much of the supporting evidence is indirect combination-therapy data.
