Safety and tolerability of all 3 regimens were similar to and consistent with previous studies in neovascular AMD.
The number of ocular serious adverse events was low and occurred largely as single cases.
All treatments were well tolerated.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Among 140 patients treated with 0.5 mg of ranibizumab, presumed endophthalmitis occurred in 2 patients (1.4%) and serious uveitis in 1 (0.7%).
The most frequent ranibizumab-associated serious ocular adverse events were intraocular inflammation (11.4%) and endophthalmitis (1.9%; 4.8% if including presumed cases).
Key serious nonocular adverse events included myocardial infarctions in the PDT-alone group (3.6%) and cerebrovascular accidents in the ranibizumab-treated group (3.8%).
CONCLUSION/APPLICATION TO CLINICAL PRACTICE: Ranibizumab + PDT was more efficacious than PDT alone for treating neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
Although ranibizumab treatment increased the risk of serious intraocular inflammation, affected patients, on average, still experienced visual acuity benefit.
Endophthalmitis and serious intraocular inflammation occurred, respectively, in 2.9% and 12.4% of ranibizumab + PDT patients and 0% of PDT-alone patients.
Incidences of serious nonocular adverse events were similar in the two treatment groups.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an OCT-guided variable-dosing regimen with intravitreal ranibizumab improved visual acuity and reduced retinal thickness over 2 years.
support_summary: Several studies report that OCT-guided or OCT-based ranibizumab retreatment regimens in neovascular AMD are associated with improved visual acuity and reduced retinal thickness, though most are not exact matches to the claimed 2-year outcome. A prospective OCT-guided variable-dosing study showed at 12 months a mean visual acuity gain of 9.3 letters and a mean central retinal thickness decrease of 178 µm with relatively few injections, directly supporting the regimen concept but only for 1 year (PMID: 17386270). A 24-month ranibizumab study with retreatment by predefined criteria found mean visual acuity improved and central retinal thickness significantly decreased at 24 months, supporting the durability of benefit, although the abstract does not explicitly state OCT-guided variable dosing (PMID: 20224469). Additional OCT-based/variable-dosing studies reported improved visual acuity over mean 16 months or significant visual acuity improvement with reduced macular thickness over 12 months, again supportive but shorter than 2 years and sometimes focused on safety or retrospective outcomes rather than the exact claim (PMID: 19730802, PMID: 22888205).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence mainly comes from longer-term or maintenance-phase ranibizumab studies showing that visual acuity often stabilizes rather than improves, and in some cohorts worsens despite anatomical benefit. A 2-year PRN study of nonsubfoveal lesions found visual acuity worsened from 20/40 at baseline to 20/60 at 2 years, with frequent recurrences and lesion growth, but this was not explicitly OCT-guided and involved extrafoveal/juxtafoveal membranes rather than the same population as the claim (PMID: 24756034). Other studies reported unchanged visual acuity during maintenance therapy or over 2 years despite stable or reduced central retinal thickness, partially contradicting the claim's visual-acuity-improvement component while still showing anatomical improvement (PMID: 22322998, PMID: 23533703, PMID: 24184931). A 5-year OCT- and VA-guided variable-dosing study also found declining visual acuity and increased central retinal thickness over time, but its much longer timeframe and likely undertreatment make it only indirectly relevant to a 2-year claim (PMID: 27354758).
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but the evidence is mixed. Support comes from studies showing OCT-guided/criteria-based ranibizumab can improve visual acuity and reduce retinal thickness, including 24-month data, yet several longer-term or maintenance-phase PRN studies show that visual acuity may merely stabilize or even worsen despite anatomical benefit, and not all studies match the exact OCT-guided 2-year regimen.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, an OCT-guided variable-dosing regimen with intravitreal ranibizumab achieved visual outcomes comparable with those reported in phase III ranibizumab trials while requiring fewer intravitreal injections.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the PrONTO study itself, which evaluated an OCT-guided variable-dosing ranibizumab regimen and explicitly concluded that 12-month visual acuity outcomes were similar to phase III studies while requiring fewer injections, with mean gain of 9.3 letters using 5.6 injections over 12 months (PMID: 17386270). A review article also summarizes PrONTO as showing that less frequent OCT-dependent variable dosing can produce comparably favorable visual outcomes relative to monthly pivotal trial results (PMID: 19668384). Additional observational evidence reports that OCT-based ranibizumab treatment yielded good visual outcomes with about 6 injections over 8-21 months and fewer injections than monthly dosing, though without a direct phase III comparison (PMID: 19730802). One cited support item is only loosely relevant because it studied individualized PRN treatment with ranibizumab versus bevacizumab and did not directly test the specific claim about phase III-comparable outcomes under an OCT-guided regimen, so this is a discrepancy in the support set (PMID: 22560423).
contradict_summary: A retrospective real-world study of ranibizumab with three loading doses followed by individualized retreatment found substantially fewer injections (mean 5.1) but only minimal 12-month visual gain relative to baseline, and explicitly stated that outcomes did not achieve the visual gains seen in randomized fixed monthly trials (PMID: 22008284). This contradicts the claim's suggestion that fewer-injection individualized treatment necessarily preserves phase III-comparable visual results. However, the study did not specifically evaluate an OCT-guided PrONTO-style regimen, so the contradiction is indirect rather than a head-to-head refutation of the exact claimed protocol (PMID: 22008284).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The key reason is that the main direct evidence comes from the OCT-guided PrONTO study itself, which explicitly reported phase III-like visual outcomes with fewer injections, whereas the contradicting evidence involves a broader individualized retreatment approach in routine practice rather than the same OCT-guided regimen.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration and central-fovea neovascularization, repeated intravitreal ranibizumab treatment produced sustained anatomic and functional benefit over 2 years.
support_summary: Several studies support that repeated intravitreal ranibizumab improves and maintains visual outcomes in neovascular AMD over long follow-up, including a large 2-year randomized sham-controlled trial showing visual acuity benefit maintained at 24 months (PMID: 17021318). Additional studies report anatomic improvement such as reduced OCT central retinal thickness or leakage along with functional benefit or vision stabilization under monthly, PRN, or OCT-guided regimens, though many only report 12-16 month outcomes rather than 2 full years (PMID: 17386270, PMID: 19730802, PMID: 23022163, PMID: 24141904, PMID: 24157918). A 2-year prospective study found significant reduction in central retinal thickness with stable long-term vision under PRN ranibizumab, which is supportive but weaker on clear functional improvement (PMID: 24184931). Reviews and modeling papers also describe ranibizumab as effective in preserving vision and reducing CNV leakage/blindness burden, but these are indirect or secondary evidence rather than direct 2-year trial data in the exact population (PMID: 21221192, PMID: 24979237, PMID: 28289548, PMID: 33500611). Overall, the strongest support comes from phase III trial evidence and OCT-based cohort studies, but there is a discrepancy because much of the supporting set does not exactly match the claim's specific central-fovea population and 2-year sustained anatomic-plus-functional endpoint.
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence includes a 2-year case series in nonsubfoveal lesions showing worsening visual acuity, lesion enlargement, universal recurrence by 2 years, and progression to subfoveal lesions in some patients, but this population differs materially from the claim's central-fovea neovascularization group (PMID: 24756034). Another study found that anatomic response in central retinal thickness was less favorable in certain subgroups and no longer statistically significant after the sixth injection, suggesting attenuation of effect rather than durable anatomic benefit in all patients (PMID: 24949110). Several contradiction items are weak or only indirectly relevant: case reports of adverse or tachyphylactic responses (PMID: 25390731, PMID: 29326855), a 12-month structural safety study that actually also reported reduced central macular thickness and therefore appears miscategorized as contradiction (PMID: 28574419), and a switch study limited to ranibizumab-unresponsive patients showing lack of functional gain before/after switching rather than directly disproving benefit in responsive patients (PMID: 33567025). Recurring themes are heterogeneity of response, nonresponse/tachyphylaxis in some individuals, and incomplete durability of anatomic response in selected subgroups. Thus, the contradiction evidence is mixed and often less directly aligned with the claim population than the main supportive trials.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The best evidence is a large 2-year randomized trial showing sustained visual benefit with ranibizumab (PMID: 17021318), supplemented by multiple studies showing accompanying anatomic improvement, whereas much of the contradictory evidence comes from nonmatching populations, selected nonresponders, or isolated case reports.

claim: In Chinese children with myopia, three novel spectacle lens designs intended to reduce peripheral hyperopic defocus did not reduce overall myopia progression compared with conventional single-vision spectacle lenses over the study period.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: Multiple later randomized or follow-up studies in Chinese children reported that newer spectacle lens designs using myopic defocus or lenslets reduced myopia progression and axial elongation versus single-vision lenses, especially DIMS lenses (PMID: 31142465, PMID: 32460315, PMID: 33731364, PMID: 37015996) and aspherical lenslet designs HAL/SAL (PMID: 35357402). These studies consistently found clinically and statistically significant reductions in refractive progression and eye growth, with sustained effects over 3 to 6 years for DIMS and no major safety concerns in long-term follow-up (PMID: 33731364, PMID: 37015996). One analysis further suggested efficacy may vary by baseline peripheral refraction but still concluded DIMS slowed progression, particularly in children with baseline hyperopic peripheral refraction (PMID: 35566423). A review/reanalysis of the two available multiple-segment lens trials also concluded these lenses provide a valuable new approach for myopia control (PMID: 37378880). However, these articles evaluate later lens designs rather than necessarily the exact three designs tested in the source study, so they contradict the broad claim about this intervention concept more than they directly overturn the specific result of that original trial.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted by the later literature. Although the original source study may have found no benefit for its three specific designs, subsequent trials in similar Chinese pediatric populations show that other newer peripheral-defocus or multiple-segment spectacle designs did reduce myopia progression compared with single-vision lenses.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Average (SE) myopic progressions over 2 years were -0.41±0.06 D in the DIMS group and -0.85±0.08 D in the SV group.
Myopia progressed 52% more slowly for children in the DIMS group compared with those in the SV group (mean difference -0.44±0.09 D, 95% CI -0.73 to -0.37, p<0.0001).
Likewise, children in the DIMS group had less axial elongation by 62% than those in the SV group (mean difference 0.34±0.04 mm, 95% CI 0.22 to 0.37, p<0.0001).
CONCLUSIONS: Daily wear of the DIMS lens significantly retarded myopia progression and axial elongation in myopic children.
CONCLUSIONS: Wearing DIMS lenses resulted in a significantly different peripheral refraction profile and RPR changes, as well as significant myopia control effects when compared with SV lenses.
Myopia control adopting myopic defocus in the midperiphery influenced peripheral refraction and slowed central myopia progression, most likely through alteration of overall retinal shape.

CONCLUSIONS: Myopia control effect was sustained in the third year in children who had used the DIMS spectacles in the previous 2 years and was also shown in the children switching from SV to DIMS lenses.

Compared with SVL, increase in axial length was slowed by a mean (SE) of 0.35 (0.05) mm for HAL and 0.18 (0.05) mm for SAL (P ≤ .001).
CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this study, HAL and SAL reduced the rate of myopia progression and axial elongation throughout 2 years, with higher efficacy for HAL.
However, DIMS lenses slowed down myopia progression and was better in myopia control for the children with baseline hyperopic RPR than the children with myopic RPR.
In the last 2.5 years, DIMS lens groups (Groups 1 and 3) had less myopia progression and axial elongation than the single vision groups (Groups 2 and 4).
The results supported that DIMS lenses provided sustained myopia control without adverse effects over the 6-year study period.
PURPOSE: Initial studies have suggested that multiple segment (MS) spectacle lenses can reduce the progression rate of childhood myopia and axial eye growth.

However, if the results were expressed in terms of efficacy over successive 6-month periods, then the two MS lenses produced broadly similar results (initial efficacy for the control of myopia progression of about 60%-80%, declining over 2 years to about 35%-55%).
CONCLUSION: Multiple segment spectacle lenses offer a valuable new approach to the control of myopia progression in children.

claim: In Chinese children with myopia who were younger and had a parental history of myopia, one novel spectacle lens design reduced myopia progression compared with conventional single-vision spectacle lenses.
support_summary: Several studies support that novel spectacle lens designs can reduce myopia progression versus single-vision lenses in children, including Chinese cohorts. A 2-year randomized trial in Chinese children found DIMS lenses slowed myopia progression by 52% and axial elongation by 62% versus single-vision lenses (PMID: 31142465), and a 1-year Chinese retrospective study also reported lower progression with DIMS, though it suggested greater benefit in older rather than younger children, which conflicts with the age-specific part of the claim (PMID: 36705721). Another 2-year randomized trial in China showed highly and slightly aspherical lenslet spectacles both reduced refractive progression and axial elongation compared with single-vision lenses, with greater efficacy for the highly aspherical design (PMID: 35357402); related exploratory data also showed reduced choroidal thinning with these lenses versus single-vision lenses (PMID: 36167484). Additional Chinese studies reported benefit of cylindrical annular refractive element lenses for axial elongation over 1 year (PMID: 36779428), and longer-term follow-up supported sustained DIMS benefit versus periods of single-vision wear (PMID: 37015996), while a comparative analysis of the Chinese DIMS and Stellest trials concluded multiple-segment lenses offer meaningful myopia-control efficacy (PMID: 37378880). The most direct support for the specific subgroup pattern in the claim comes from a non-Chinese Israeli trial, where a novel spectacle lens slowed progression especially in younger children and those with two myopic parents, so it supports the subgroup concept but not the Chinese population specifically (PMID: 37690499).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported, because multiple studies—especially randomized trials in Chinese children—show that novel spectacle lens designs reduce myopia progression compared with single-vision lenses. However, the claim's specific emphasis on younger Chinese children with parental myopia is only indirectly supported: one non-Chinese study matches that subgroup pattern, whereas one Chinese DIMS study suggested stronger efficacy in older children.

claim: Among Chinese children aged 6 to 16 years with myopia, the rate of myopia progression did not differ significantly between conventional single-vision spectacle lenses and the novel spectacle lens designs overall.
support_summary: One supporting item does not directly test the claim about myopia progression. The study on DIMS versus single-vision spectacles reported no statistically significant differences in 2-year visual function changes between groups, suggesting similar visual-function outcomes but not explicitly similar myopia progression rates (PMID: 32879767). This makes it only indirect and relatively weak support for the claim.
contradict_summary: Most contradicting evidence directly reports that novel optical designs slow myopia progression or axial elongation compared with single-vision spectacles in Chinese children. Randomized or comparative studies found significantly less refractive progression and/or axial elongation with DIMS lenses (PMID: 31142465; PMID: 36705721; PMID: 37015996; PMID: 38041675), highly or slightly aspherical lenslet spectacles (PMID: 35357402), and CARE spectacle lenses (PMID: 39548605), while one contact-lens study also showed slower progression than single-vision spectacles but is not strictly about spectacle-lens designs and is therefore only indirectly relevant to the claim wording (PMID: 22039230). One CARE study showed significant reduction in axial elongation but not a clearly significant refractive-error difference, so it partially rather than fully contradicts the claim (PMID: 36779428). Another item compares HAL with DIMS rather than novel lenses with single-vision controls, so its placement as contradictory to this specific claim is a discrepancy despite showing differences among novel designs (PMID: 36810369). A 3-year follow-up also suggests sustained DIMS benefit versus historical controls, but it is less direct because it lacks a concurrent SV comparison in year 3 (PMID: 33731364).
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence consists of multiple Chinese pediatric spectacle-lens studies showing significant reductions in myopia progression and/or axial elongation with novel lens designs versus single-vision spectacles, whereas the lone supporting study addresses visual function rather than progression itself.

claim: Short-term oral saffron supplementation in patients with early age-related macular degeneration improves retinal flicker sensitivity compared with placebo or baseline, as shown by increased macular focal electroretinogram amplitude and reduced modulation thresholds.
support_summary: Multiple studies in early or mild/moderate AMD report improved retinal electrophysiologic function after oral saffron, especially by about 3 months. Longitudinal AMD cohorts found that after 3 months, fERG sensitivity and/or amplitude improved significantly versus baseline, with benefits then remaining stable during longer follow-up (PMID: 22852021; PMID: 24067115). A randomized placebo-controlled AMD trial also found significantly increased ERG results at 3 months with saffron versus placebo, though it did not specifically report flicker sensitivity or modulation thresholds and effects weakened by 6 months (PMID: 28289690). Another randomized crossover trial showed modest improvement in visual function with saffron versus placebo, including reduced mfERG latency and improved mfERG response density in some subgroups, which is only indirect support for the specific claim metrics (PMID: 30343354). One mechanistic paper states in its background that saffron improves retinal flicker sensitivity in AMD patients, but this is not primary clinical evidence for the claimed trial outcome and should be treated as secondary support only (PMID: 26739703).
contradict_summary: The only contradicting item is a randomized placebo-controlled study in ABCA4-related Stargardt macular dystrophy, not early AMD, so its disease mismatch limits direct contradiction of the claim (PMID: 31618812). In that Stargardt cohort, fERG amplitude was unchanged after saffron, supporting lack of benefit in that condition rather than in early AMD specifically (PMID: 31618812). Thus, this evidence raises caution about generalizing retinal benefits across macular diseases but does not strongly refute the early-AMD claim.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from AMD studies showing significant improvements in fERG-derived sensitivity and amplitude versus baseline and some placebo-controlled ERG benefit at 3 months, while the lone negative study was conducted in a different retinal disease and is therefore only weakly contradictory.

support_highlights.snippet: In a previous randomized clinical trial (Falsini et al. (2010)), it was shown that short-term Saffron supplementation improves retinal flicker sensitivity in early age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

After three months of supplementation, mean fERG sensitivity improved by 0.3 log units compared to baseline values (P < 0.01), and mean visual acuity improved by two Snellen lines compared to baseline values (0.75 to 0.9, P < 0.01).
After three months of supplementation, mean fERG amplitude and fERG sensitivity improved significantly when compared to baseline values (p < 0.01).

Experimental studies demonstrated that saffron protects photoreceptors from light-induced damage preserving both retinal morphology and visual function and improves retinal flicker sensitivity in AMD patients.

Finally, there was a significant increase in ERG findings between the groups with wet AMD at 3 months after treatment (P = 0.01), but these changes decreased at 6 months after treatment (P = 0.213).

Amongst participants on AREDS supplements, mean BCVA improved 0.73 letters p = 0.006) and mean-pooled mfERG response density improved 2.8% (p = 0.038).

claim: Short-term oral saffron supplementation in patients with early age-related macular degeneration has a beneficial functional effect on retinal response, whereas placebo supplementation does not produce a similar improvement.
support_summary: Most supporting evidence indicates that oral saffron supplementation improves retinal functional measures in AMD, with the strongest direct evidence coming from randomized placebo-controlled trials. A crossover RCT in mild/moderate AMD found that 3 months of saffron improved best-corrected visual acuity and reduced pooled mfERG latency versus placebo, with additional mfERG response density benefit among participants also taking AREDS supplements (PMID: 30343354). Another double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial reported significantly increased ERG findings at 3 months in both dry and wet AMD groups receiving saffron versus placebo, although some effects were less evident by 6 months (PMID: 28289690). Open-label longitudinal studies in early AMD consistently found that after 3 months of saffron, fERG sensitivity/amplitude and visual acuity improved from baseline and remained stable over longer follow-up, but these studies lacked a placebo comparator and therefore only partially address the claim (PMID: 22852021; PMID: 24067115). One older mechanistic study on crocin analogs supports biological plausibility by showing improved retinal/choroidal blood flow and retinal function recovery, but it is indirect and not a placebo-controlled trial in early AMD patients receiving saffron supplementation (PMID: 10229492).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. This is mainly because two randomized placebo-controlled trials reported short-term improvements in retinal functional outcomes with saffron compared with placebo, while the remaining studies provide consistent but less definitive baseline-controlled or mechanistic support.

support_highlights.snippet: Mean BCVA improved 0.69 letters (p = 0.001) and mean-pooled mfERG latency reduced 0.17 ms (p = 0.04) on saffron compared to placebo.
Amongst participants on AREDS supplements, mean BCVA improved 0.73 letters p = 0.006) and mean-pooled mfERG response density improved 2.8% (p = 0.038).
Saffron supplementation modestly improved visual function in participants with AMD, including those using AREDS supplements.
Crocin analogs isolated from Crocus sativus L. were found to significantly increase the blood flow in the retina and choroid and to facilitate retinal function recovery.
These results indicated that crocin analogs could be used to treat ischemic retinopathy and/or age-related macular degeneration.

After three months of supplementation, mean fERG sensitivity improved by 0.3 log units compared to baseline values (P < 0.01), and mean visual acuity improved by two Snellen lines compared to baseline values (0.75 to 0.9, P < 0.01).
These changes remained stable over the follow-up period.
After three months of supplementation, mean fERG amplitude and fERG sensitivity improved significantly when compared to baseline values (p < 0.01).

The present results indicate that the functional effect of Saffron supplementation in individual AMD patients is not related to the major risk genotypes of disease.
Sixty patients with wet or dry age-related macular degeneration (AMD) were randomly assigned to receive oral saffron 30 mg/d or placebo supplementation for 6 months.
However, there was a statistically significant increase in ERG results between the groups at 3 months after treatment (P = 0.027).
Finally, there was a significant increase in ERG findings between the groups with wet AMD at 3 months after treatment (P = 0.01), but these changes decreased at 6 months after treatment (P = 0.213).
Daily supplementation with 30 mg of saffron for 6 months may result in a mid-term, significant improvement in retinal function in patients with AMD.

claim: In eyes with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, reticular drusen are a common phenotypic feature and are more readily detected with confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscopy imaging than with standard fundus photography.
support_summary: Two studies support that reticular drusen are common in AMD eyes with geographic atrophy and are well visualized with advanced imaging. In a multimodal imaging comparison, reticular drusen were seen by SD-OCT and FAF in 100% of eyes with AMD-associated GA, supporting their commonality in GA, although this study did not directly compare cSLO with standard fundus photography for reticular drusen detection (PMID: 22003107). A longitudinal GA cohort study used cSLO fundus autofluorescence to identify and map reticular drusen over time, showing lesion boundaries were clearly detectable and that affected area enlarged longitudinally, which supports detectability by cSLO-based imaging but again not a direct head-to-head comparison with photography (PMID: 23633663). Overall, the supporting evidence is strongest for reticular drusen being a frequent phenotypic feature in GA secondary to AMD, and only indirect for superior detection by cSLO versus standard fundus photography.
contradict_summary: The contradicting evidence is mixed and includes a likely discrepancy in categorization. One study of high-risk early/intermediate AMD found reticular drusen in 44% of eyes and stated they appear less common than in eyes with geographic atrophy; it also reported they were always visible on near-infrared reflectance and less often on color images, which actually aligns more with the claim's imaging component than contradicts it (PMID: 25795913). Another community-based diagnostic accuracy study reported that when IR+OCT was the reference standard, color fundus photography had relatively low sensitivity for reticular pseudodrusen (76.2%) and GA (77.8%), supporting better detection with confocal IR/OCT-type imaging, but it was not restricted to GA secondary to AMD and did not directly study the specific phenotype/context of the claim (PMID: 30575074). Thus, the nominally contradicting set provides little true contradiction and is weakened by scope differences and probable misclassification.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence consistently indicates that reticular drusen are common in AMD-associated geographic atrophy and that cSLO-based multimodal imaging detects them well, while the supposed contradictory studies either actually align with improved non-photographic detection or do not directly address the exact GA-specific claim.

support_highlights.snippet: Reticular drusen could be visualized by SD-OCT and FAF imaging in 52.6% of the eyes with early AMD and in 100% of the eyes with GA, whereas this drusen phenotype did not manifest in eyes with CACD.
RESULTS: RDR lesion boundaries were clearly detectable in all directions within three-field FAF composite images in 16 eyes of 10 patients at both baseline and final visits.
CONCLUSIONS: In vivo cSLO FAF imaging allows for both qualitative and quantitative mapping of longitudinal changes of RDR areas within a relatively short time period.
Continuous enlargement of the affected retinal area indicates disease progression with regard to this phenotypic characteristic associated with GA in AMD.

claim: In patients with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, reticular drusen detection is associated with older age and female sex, but not with the size of the geographic atrophy lesion.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: One study examined reticular pseudodrusen/reticular drusen in geographic atrophy but did not directly assess the claim's demographic associations with older age or female sex. Instead, it reported that eyes with GA and reticular pseudodrusen had thinner subfoveal choroids than GA eyes without reticular pseudodrusen, and that in eyes without reticular pseudodrusen, larger GA size correlated with lower choroidal thickness (PMID: 26057754). This does not directly show that reticular drusen detection is unrelated to GA lesion size, and the evidence item is only indirectly relevant to the claim, so its placement as contradiction is somewhat discrepant.
conclusion: The evidence is overall unclear, with no direct supporting studies provided and only one indirectly relevant study that does not test the key age- and sex-related parts of the claim. Thus, the claim is not meaningfully contradicted, but it is also not substantiated by the supplied evidence set.

contradict_highlights.snippet: After controlling for age and axial length, the mean CT was significantly thinner in the GA group with RPD (213.7 ± 53.1 µm) than in the GA group without RPD (335.3 ± 123.2 µm; P = .001).
In eyes without RPD, there was a correlation between the increasing size of GA and a decrease in CT measurements.
Subfoveal choroidal thinning in eyes with nonexudative AMD was associated with the presence of RPD.
In the absence of RPD, CT only decreased as the size of GA increased.

claim: In eyes with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, reticular drusen are most often located superior to the fovea.
support_summary: One population-based longitudinal study reported that soft indistinct or reticular drusen were most likely to develop in the superior or temporal quadrants of the macula, suggesting a tendency toward superior distribution but not specifically establishing that they are most often superior to the fovea in eyes with geographic atrophy secondary to AMD (PMID: 15223787). The same study also found that lesions closer to the fovea may be associated with higher risk of late ARM, but its abstract does not directly analyze reticular drusen location specifically within the subgroup of eyes with geographic atrophy (PMID: 15223787). Thus, the evidence is only indirect and only partially aligned with the claim.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is weakly supported overall because the only available study suggests reticular drusen preferentially develop in the superior or temporal macula, but it does not directly examine eyes with geographic atrophy secondary to AMD or specifically show that reticular drusen are most often superior to the fovea. The evidence base here is therefore limited and indirect.

support_highlights.snippet: Compared with other quadrants, drusen greater than 125 micro m in diameter and soft indistinct or reticular drusen were most likely to develop in the superior or temporal quadrants, whereas pigmentary abnormalities were most likely to occur in the nasal or superior quadrants.
In general, large drusen, soft indistinct drusen, and pigmentary abnormalities were more likely to develop in the inner circle versus the central and outer circles.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation increases macular pigment optical density compared with placebo.
support_summary: Multiple randomized placebo-controlled trials in AMD/ARMD patients report that lutein-containing supplementation increases macular pigment optical density (MPOD) versus placebo or no supplementation, including the Veterans LAST trial where MPOD rose by about 0.09 log units with lutein while placebo showed no significant change (PMID: 15117055) and LAST II showing MPOD increased with supplementation but declined slightly without it (PMID: 17478338). Several later RCTs in early AMD also found significant MPOD gains in active lutein groups, often with dose-response effects and no notable placebo change, including 20 mg lutein and lutein/zeaxanthin regimens over 48 weeks to 2 years (PMID: 22858124, PMID: 23312760, PMID: 25228440, PMID: 25815324). Additional supportive but less direct studies include lutein combined with DHA or zeaxanthin, where supplemented AMD groups had greater MPOD increases than placebo/control, though the independent effect of lutein alone cannot always be isolated (PMID: 23434908, PMID: 23519529, PMID: 22835510). Some SUPPORT items are only indirect or not placebo-controlled, such as observational/review/pilot studies or studies in healthy subjects rather than AMD patients, so they provide weaker relevance to the exact claim (PMID: 11157880, PMID: 12058502, PMID: 12672909, PMID: 17552428, PMID: 26950968). Overall, the dominant theme across the strongest evidence is that lutein-containing supplementation, especially at higher doses or among those with lower baseline MPOD, increases MPOD in AMD patients compared with placebo/no supplementation.
contradict_summary: Two randomized studies reported no statistically significant MPOD increase despite carotenoid exposure. One trial in healthy volunteers consuming lutein- or zeaxanthin-enriched eggs or a lutein-enriched beverage found substantial serum carotenoid increases but no macular pigment density change; however, this does not match the claim population because participants were healthy volunteers, not AMD patients (PMID: 24675775). A second randomized double-masked multicenter study including patients with neovascular AMD in one eye found no statistically significant MPOD increase in supplemented groups, but the study mixed retinal-pathology and non-pathology groups and used Visucam-based MPOD measurement, which the authors suggest may limit detection (PMID: 28336284). Thus, the contradicting evidence is limited in quantity and somewhat heterogeneous, with one study only partially applicable to AMD.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence consists of several randomized placebo-controlled AMD trials consistently showing lutein-containing supplementation increases MPOD, whereas the contradictory evidence is sparse, includes one non-AMD population, and one mixed-population study with null findings.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in visual acuity compared with placebo.
support_summary: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in early AMD found that although lutein/zeaxanthin increased serum levels, macular pigment, and some contrast sensitivity measures, there were no statistical changes in best-spectacle corrected visual acuity during the trial, supporting lack of clear acuity benefit versus placebo (PMID: 25815324). A review article states that a definite association between lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has not yet been shown, despite limited data suggesting possible visual-function improvement, which indirectly supports the claim that visual acuity benefit is not clearly established (PMID: 18574012). Overall, the supporting theme is that biologic and some functional effects are seen, but clear improvement in visual acuity specifically is not demonstrated.
contradict_summary: The strongest contradictory evidence is the Veterans LAST randomized placebo-controlled trial, which reported that Snellen equivalent visual acuity improved by 5.4 letters with lutein alone and 3.5 letters with lutein plus antioxidants, while the placebo group had no significant changes, directly arguing against the claim (PMID: 15117055). Another randomized placebo-controlled trial in early AMD found only a trend toward better BCVA at 48 weeks, not a clear statistically significant acuity improvement, though contrast sensitivity improved significantly; this partially contradicts the claim mainly on broader visual-function outcomes rather than definitive acuity benefit (PMID: 22858124). There is also a discrepancy across studies, because PMID: 22858124 and PMID: 25815324 appear closely related in design/population yet differ in emphasis, with one reporting a BCVA trend and the other stating no statistical BCVA change.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward the claim being supported: larger/placebo-controlled evidence and review-level evidence suggest no clear visual acuity improvement, even when other visual measures improve. However, one placebo-controlled trial reported measurable acuity gains versus placebo, so the claim is not definitively settled.

support_highlights.snippet: No statistical changes of BCVA or FRT were observed during the trial.
Limited data have suggested that supplementation may also improve visual function.

A definite association between lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has yet to be shown; however, it may still be an appropriate cautionary measure for patients at high risk for developing AMD.

Patients who received the placebo (Group 3) had no significant changes in any of the measured findings.
In this study, visual function is improved with lutein alone or lutein together with other nutrients.
At 48 weeks, a trend toward improvement was seen in BCVA, and there was a significant between-group difference in CS at 3 and 6 cycles/degree between the 20-mg lutein group and the placebo group.
Among patients with early AMD, supplementation with lutein and zeaxanthin improved macular pigment, which played a causative role in boosting visual function and might prevent the progression of AMD.

claim: In patients with age-related macular degeneration, lutein supplementation does not produce a clear improvement in macular function measured by microperimetry compared with placebo, although greater increases in macular pigment optical density are associated with greater improvements in visual function.
support_summary: Supportive evidence is limited and indirect. A review notes that lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation increases plasma levels and is positively associated with macular pigment optical density (MPOD), but clinical benefit remains unproven and visual-function data are limited, which is broadly consistent with the claim's cautious interpretation (PMID: 18574012). Another randomized study in subjects including neovascular AMD found no statistically significant increase in MPOD with carotenoid supplementation, though it did not assess microperimetry or placebo effects on macular function in the same way as the claim (PMID: 28336284). Overall, these studies support uncertainty about clear functional benefit more than they directly confirm the specific microperimetry-based statement. There is also a discrepancy because neither study directly addresses the claimed association between greater MPOD increase and greater visual-function improvement in AMD measured against placebo.
contradict_summary: Several randomized or prospective studies report that lutein-containing supplementation improves visual outcomes in AMD or related early macular disease, often alongside increased MPOD, which argues against a blanket statement of no clear functional improvement. Placebo-controlled trials found significant gains in contrast sensitivity, mfERG response densities, or visual acuity with lutein or lutein/zeaxanthin, and some reported positive correlations between MPOD increases and improved visual measures (PMID: 25815324, PMID: 15117055, PMID: 22835510, PMID: 22858124, PMID: 27064326). Additional non-placebo or mixed-formulation studies also reported improved retinal sensitivity or contrast sensitivity, though they are less directly comparable to the claim because they did not use microperimetry-vs-placebo specifically or included DHA/meso-zeaxanthin combinations (PMID: 21850440, PMID: 23434908, PMID: 24887490). A recurring theme is that benefits are more evident for some functional endpoints than others, with some studies showing no change in BCVA or dark adaptation despite improved MPOD or contrast sensitivity (PMID: 25815324, PMID: 27064326). Thus, contradiction is substantial but partly weakened by endpoint differences from the claim's specific focus on microperimetry.
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported by the cited literature. Although a small amount of indirect evidence supports uncertainty about clear benefit, multiple controlled studies in AMD report improvements in visual function and/or positive associations between MPOD increases and functional gains after lutein-containing supplementation, even if they do not all use microperimetry specifically.

support_highlights.snippet: Increased dietary intake of or supplementation with lutein and zeaxanthin was found to result in increased plasma levels, which were positively and significantly associated with macular pigment optical density.
Limited data have suggested that supplementation may also improve visual function.

A definite association between lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation and clinical benefit has yet to be shown; however, it may still be an appropriate cautionary measure for patients at high risk for developing AMD.

Statistically significant (P < 0.05) improvements in letter contrast sensitivity were seen at all spatial frequencies (except 1.2 cycles per degree) in Group 3, and at low spatial frequencies in Groups 1 and 2.

CONCLUSION: Augmentation of the macular pigment optical density across its spatial profile and enhancements in contrast sensitivity were best achieved after supplementation with a formulation containing high doses of meso-zeaxanthin in combination with lutein and zeaxanthin.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab produced similar improvements in visual acuity over a 2-year period.
support_summary: Several comparative studies report broadly similar visual acuity outcomes with bevacizumab and ranibizumab in neovascular AMD, including a 2-year comparison showing gains of 7.0 vs 9.2 letters with no significant difference (PMID: 27162727). Real-world registry data also found that the 2-year risk of poor vision did not depend on whether patients received ranibizumab or bevacizumab (PMID: 28141748). Additional shorter-term or indirect studies found similar visual acuity gains or no apparent differences between drugs, but many did not specifically assess a full 2-year head-to-head comparison (PMID: 19384786, PMID: 19590252, PMID: 19696701, PMID: 30810619). Some SUPPORT items are only indirect support because they address 1-year outcomes, mixed anti-VEGF cohorts without separate drug comparisons, or review-level statements rather than direct 2-year randomized evidence (PMID: 22211145, PMID: 24637667).
contradict_summary: Contradicting studies mainly come from switch analyses or subgroup analyses rather than direct general-population 2-year head-to-head comparisons. In a switch study, visual acuity decreased after changing from ranibizumab to bevacizumab, suggesting a possible small disadvantage for bevacizumab, although disease progression could also explain the change (PMID: 25196907). A retrospective subgroup study in patients with incomplete posterior vitreous detachment found greater visual and anatomic improvement with ranibizumab than bevacizumab over 12 months, but this applies to a specific subgroup and not necessarily all neovascular AMD patients (PMID: 26692256). One CATT-related cohort found bevacizumab associated with a higher risk of sustained visual acuity loss over 2 years, while another cited item is actually more supportive than contradictory because it states monthly bevacizumab was noninferior to monthly ranibizumab at 1 year, indicating a categorization discrepancy (PMID: 24875610, PMID: 21961847). Another switching study suggested ranibizumab-only or bevacizumab-to-ranibizumab groups performed better than ranibizumab-to-bevacizumab, but this was retrospective and confounded by switching context (PMID: 23516253).
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest directly relevant evidence includes 2-year comparative data and real-world 2-year outcomes showing no significant visual acuity difference between ranibizumab and bevacizumab, whereas much of the contradictory evidence comes from subgroup or treatment-switch settings that are less directly applicable to the general claim.

support_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: Visual acuity at 2y bevacizumab patients gained 7.0 letters and ranibizumab 9.2 (P=0.31, 95% CI -6.4 to 2.0).
The risk of having a visual acuity lower than 60 letters does not depend on the choice of treatment drug.

RESULTS: The treatment with Avastin is completed in 319 eyes with an average improvement of the visual acuity of 5.1 letters after 3.3 injections, and with Lucentis in 226 eyes with an average improvement of the visual acuity of 6.4 letters after 3.4 injections (p = 0.24; one way ANOVA).
CONCLUSION: Both of the drugs allow the drying of the macular findings in the great majority of the cases after a short time and lead to a quite similar improvement of the visual acuity.
The mean logMAR equivalent of BCVA at 1 month after the injection improved by 0.18 in the bevacizumab group (p = 0.009) and by 0.13 in the ranibizumab group (p = 0.004).
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab and ranibizumab treatments resulted in similar gains in visual acuity and reduction in macular thickness, documented each month following injection.
Best-obtained visual acuity during treatment was 20/63 with bevacizumab and 20/63 with ranibizumab (P = 0.5).
Mean visual acuity at the last ranibizumab follow-up visit was 20/80 (P = 0.49).
CONCLUSION: In this subset of patients with neovascular AMD switched from bevacizumab to ranibizumab therapy, there were no apparent differences in visual acuity outcomes or injection rates.
Many studies have indicated that Ranibizumab and Bevacizumab are of roughly equal short-term efficacy and safety, Bevacizumab is an attractive alternative to Ranibizumab due to its lower cost.
After 1 year of follow-up, we found statistically significant improvements in visual acuity and central macular thickness reduction in all groups.
However, we found no statistically significant differences between the three groups.
CONCLUSIONS: The bi-weekly follow-up was effective and we found no significant differences in efficacy or safety between the treatments with ranibizumab and bevacizumab.

contradict_highlights.snippet: After the switch, there was a statistically significant decrease in BCVA to 51.7 letters (p < 0.001).

CONCLUSION: Switching patients to bevacizumab may have a minor negative effect on the initial gain obtained with ranibizumab; however the degenerative history of wet AMD could explain this small variation in visual acuity.

In subjects with vitreous attachment to the fovea, the ranibizumab cohort had a greater improvement in visual acuity (0.18 ± 0.1 vs 0.04 ± 0.11 logMAR) (P = 0.0176) and a greater reduction in macular thickness (93.4 ± 32.2 μm vs 30.3 ± 28.3 μm) (P = 0.0064) compared with the bevacizumab cohort.
CONCLUSIONS: Neovascular AMD patients with vitreous attachment to the fovea may have better visual and anatomic outcomes when treated with ranibizumab compared with bevacizumab.
Visual acuity results for bevacizumab as needed were inconclusive for noninferiority relative to monthly administration of either drug.
After switching, BCVA continuously improved in the bevacizumab to ranibizumab group, but stayed stable in the ranibizumab to bevacizumab group.
The BCVA worsened in 20% of eyes in the bevacizumab to ranibizumab group and in 40% of eyes in the ranibizumab to bevacizumab group.
