Risk was 3% higher among eyes treated with bevacizumab.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, as-needed dosing produced less improvement in visual acuity than monthly dosing, whether started at treatment initiation or after switching from monthly treatment.
support_summary: Several studies support that less frequent or PRN dosing yields smaller visual acuity gains than regular dosing in neovascular AMD. A retrospective pooled analysis of phase III/IIIb ranibizumab trials found that after 3 monthly loading doses, continued monthly dosing produced more delayed ≥15-letter responders and better maintenance of gains than switching to less frequent regimens, directly matching the claim's post-switch component (PMID: 23251787). Other studies reported that PRN from baseline or after loading was associated with smaller gains or lower responder rates than more regular schedules, including loading-dose versus PRN comparisons (PMID: 20693484), PRN from treatment initiation not achieving the efficacy of regular monthly injections (PMID: 20890885), and PRN performing worse than bimonthly aflibercept (PMID: 28402983). Additional indirect or lower-specificity support comes from clinician-determined retreatment appearing less effective than trial regimens (PMID: 20937739), modeling suggesting better vision-related outcomes with monthly than PRN ranibizumab (PMID: 24979237), routine-practice data favoring treat-and-extend over PRN though this is not a monthly comparison (PMID: 38976148), and regular anti-VEGF treatment outperforming scarce treatment in poor-vision nAMD (PMID: 39167570); these latter studies are supportive but not exact matches to the claim.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is mixed and includes some studies that may be better viewed as neutral rather than truly contradictory. A randomized trial found ranibizumab treat-and-extend was not worse than monthly dosing over 24 months, but this addresses T&E rather than PRN/as-needed dosing, so it is a discrepancy in categorization relative to the claim (PMID: 31917441). A retrospective comparison of monthly versus variable dosing after 3 loading injections found similar visual acuity outcomes at 12 months, with only a nonsignificant trend favoring monthly treatment, which weakens but does not clearly refute the claim (PMID: 21926941). One PRN ranibizumab case series showed visual acuity improvement/stabilization with PRN treatment from baseline but did not directly compare against monthly dosing, so it is only indirect contradiction at most (PMID: 21400056). An expert commentary on CATT stated that PRN bevacizumab visual acuity results were inconclusive for noninferiority versus monthly dosing and suggested modified monthly regimens are feasible, which is not a direct contradiction and is partly consistent with possible inferiority of PRN (PMID: 21961847).
conclusion: Overall, the statement is more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most claim-relevant evidence shows better visual acuity outcomes with continued monthly dosing than with switching to less frequent/PRN regimens and generally poorer gains with PRN strategies, whereas the opposing studies are mostly indirect, neutral, or address treat-and-extend rather than true as-needed dosing.

support_highlights.snippet: After three monthly loading doses, continued monthly dosing resulted in further gains, as there were more delayed 15-letter responders at month 12 (14.7-16.1%) than with less frequent dosing (5.0-6.0%).
Monthly dosing also resulted in more patients maintaining VA gains at later time points.
Continued monthly dosing resulted in a greater percentage of patients gaining ≥15 letters than with switching to less frequent dosing regimens.

At 24 months, patients on the T&E regimen experienced significantly greater mean (95% confidence interval) improvement in VA than those on PRN (+ 4.2 [3.1, 5.2] vs. + 1.3 [0.1, 2.6] letters; p < 0.001), with more injections (14.9 standard deviation(SD) 4.3) vs. 9.8(SD 4.3); p < 0.001).
Eyes treated with a T&E regimen had better VA outcomes from VEGF inhibitors than eyes treated PRN.
However, twice as many patients improved visual acuity by 15 or more letters in the LD group (29.8% in the LD group compared with 12.9% in the PRN group (p=0.01)).
This study showed that standard protocols used for OCT-guided retreatment achieved smaller mean gains in vision than those obtained with monthly ranibizumab administration.
Further, loading doses of ranibizumab resulted in more visual gains than the PRN protocol.
Mean visual acuity change was an increase of 1.3 letters (95% confidence interval -2.7 to +5.3), and difference between angiographic patterns did not reach statistical significance (p=0.30).

As-needed treatment from the start achieved stabilization of visual acuity and a moderate decrease of retinal thickness with a low number of injections, but did not achieve the same efficacy as regular monthly injections.
CONCLUSIONS: Clinician-determined re-treatment after a three-dose initiation phase appears to be less effective in improving BCVA than in randomised controlled trials.
Monthly 0.5 mg intravitreal ranibizumab would reduce incident blindness by 72% (95% simulation interval, 70-74%). Ranibizumab given as needed would reduce incident blindness by 68% (64-71%).

Without treatment, 4846 individuals would become visually impaired over 2 years; this proportion would be reduced by 37% (34-39%) with monthly intravitreal ranibizumab, and by 28% (23-33%) with ranibizumab given as needed.
BCVA was significantly improved only in the bimonthly group at 12 months.
BCVA showed significant improvement only in the bimonthly group but not in the PRN group at 12 months.

Improvements in visual acuity, reduction in central subfield macular thickness, and maximal height of choroidal neovascularization were significantly favorable in the regular treatment group at Year 1, and central subfield macular thickness was significantly decreased at Year 2.
Survival analysis revealed that the regular treatment group had significantly greater preservation of visual acuity and lesion size than that in the scarce treatment group.

Maintaining intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor treatment for patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration and poor vision showed significant advantages in visual acuity and lesion size stability and reduced the incidence of new subretinal hemorrhage, which suggests preservation of paracentral vision.

contradict_highlights.snippet: At 12 months, the mean logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (logMAR) visual acuity improved by 0.078 logMAR units (P = 0.046) and the mean central retinal thickness decreased by 85.7 μm (P < 0.001).
Thirty of 41 eyes (73.2%) avoided any loss of vision, and 20 eyes (48.8 %) showed improved visual acuity.
Ranibizumab administered on an as-needed basis may stabilize visual acuity in patients with neovascular AMD.
The mean (SD) BCVA improvement was not worse with the T&E arm, 6.8 (14.1) letters vs 6.0 (12.6) letters, compared with the monthly arm (difference, 0.9; 95% CI, -1.6 to 3.3; P = .21).

There was a gain of 15 or more letters in 25.5% of the T&E group and 23.1% of the monthly treatment group (difference, 2.4%; 95% CI, -6.8% to 11.6%; P = .59) and a loss of 15 or more letters in 6.5% of the T&E group and 5.8% of the monthly treatment group (difference, -0.7%; 95% CI, -9.9% to 8.5%; P = .85).

These findings suggest that change in vision from baseline is not worse with a T&E compared with a monthly regimen of ranibizumab for nAMD through 24 months, achieving clinically meaningful improvements in BCVA despite fewer injections and visits.

At 12 months the median number of injections were 12 and 8, respectively, and the mean change in Snellen visual acuity was an improvement of 0.27 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution in the monthly treated group versus 0.21 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution improvement in the variable-dosing group (P = 0.53).
In the monthly treated group 96.8% of eyes lost <0.3 logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution versus 96.6% of eyes in the variable-dosing group (P = 1.0).
We were able to show that in our clinical setting patients achieved similar visual acuity results with either monthly injections or with a variable-dosing protocol.
Visual acuity results for bevacizumab as needed were inconclusive for noninferiority relative to monthly administration of either drug.
The trial supports use of either drug as primary therapy and suggests that modification of monthly dosing regimens is feasible.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, ranibizumab and bevacizumab had similar rates of death and arteriothrombotic events, but bevacizumab was associated with more systemic serious adverse events.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the CATT randomized noninferiority trial, which reported similar rates of death, myocardial infarction, and stroke between bevacizumab and ranibizumab, but a higher proportion of serious systemic adverse events with bevacizumab (24.1% vs 19.0%) in neovascular AMD (PMID: 21526923). A review/commentary on CATT also notes that bevacizumab had a higher reported rate of serious adverse events, although it does not add direct data on death or arteriothrombotic events and emphasizes that safety findings need further analysis (PMID: 21970950). One retrospective bevacizumab-versus-control study found more hospital admissions but no increase in arteriothrombotic admissions with bevacizumab; however, it did not compare bevacizumab with ranibizumab, so it only indirectly supports the SAE portion of the claim and not the head-to-head comparison (PMID: 23569355). Overall, support is driven mainly by one large randomized trial, while the other supporting items are weaker or only partially relevant.
contradict_summary: Several head-to-head studies reported no significant difference in serious systemic adverse events between bevacizumab and ranibizumab, including IVAN (PMID: 22578446), GEFAL (PMID: 23916488), LUCAS 2-year results (PMID: 26477842), an Austrian randomized trial (PMID: 23292928), and a retrospective cohort analysis (PMID: 23255862). Some studies also suggested fewer arteriothrombotic events with bevacizumab rather than similar rates, notably IVAN and LUCAS, which conflicts with the claim’s statement of similar event rates (PMID: 22578446; PMID: 25227499). A large population-based nested case-control study found no significant differences between the drugs for ischemic stroke, myocardial infarction, venous thromboembolism, or heart failure, contradicting the assertion that bevacizumab causes more systemic serious adverse events (PMID: 22763393). Several smaller retrospective studies reported no serious complications or no systemic adverse events, but many of these are limited by size and often focus on ocular rather than systemic safety, so they are weaker contradictions or only partially relevant (PMID: 23040443; PMID: 23638418; PMID: 24558597). One listed contradicting commentary is actually mixed, because it acknowledges higher observed serious adverse events with bevacizumab while noting similar cardiac and neurologic event counts, reflecting discrepancy within the evidence set rather than clear contradiction (PMID: 21961847).
conclusion: The statement is overall mixed but somewhat contradicted by the broader evidence base. Although the large CATT trial strongly supports the claim, multiple other comparative trials and observational studies generally found no significant excess of systemic serious adverse events with bevacizumab and in some cases reported fewer arteriothrombotic events, so the totality of evidence does not consistently uphold the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: Rates of death, myocardial infarction, and stroke were similar for patients receiving either bevacizumab or ranibizumab (P>0.20).

The proportion of patients with serious systemic adverse events (primarily hospitalizations) was higher with bevacizumab than with ranibizumab (24.1% vs. 19.0%; risk ratio, 1.29; 95% confidence interval, 1.01 to 1.66), with excess events broadly distributed in disease categories not identified in previous studies as areas of concern.

While CATT provided further evidence for the efficacy of bevacizumab treatment, differences in adverse events between the two treatments (e.g. a higher rate of serious adverse events with bevacizumab compared with ranibizumab) were reported; however, these still have to be analysed, with the larger sample sizes of previous ranibizumab studies needing to be taken into account.
There were significantly more hospital admissions among bevacizumab-treated patients than in the control group (P = 0.039).
Sub-analysis of hospitalizations due to arteriothrombotic causes did not reveal a statistically significant difference between groups (P = 0.629).

contradict_highlights.snippet: No death or thromboembolic events were observed.
Intraocular bevacizumab appears to be well tolerated for the treatment of a variety of retinal and choroidal vascular diseases.
Fewer participants receiving bevacizumab had an arteriothrombotic event or heart failure (odds ratio [OR], 0.23; 95% CI, 0.05 to 1.07; P = 0.03).
There was no difference between drugs in the proportion experiencing a serious systemic adverse event (OR, 1.35; 95% CI, 0.80 to 2.27; P = 0.25).
Other outcomes are consistent with the drugs and treatment regimens having similar efficacy and safety.

Similarly, a secondary analysis of exclusive users of bevacizumab or ranibizumab showed no differences in risk between the two drugs (adjusted odds ratios for bevacizumab relative to ranibizumab of 1.03 (0.67 to 1.60) for ischaemic stroke, 1.23 (0.85 to 1.77) for acute myocardial infarction, 0.92 (0.51 to 1.69) for venous thromboembolism, and 1.35 (0.93 to 1.95) for congestive heart failure).

CONCLUSIONS: Intravitreal injections of bevacizumab and ranibizumab were not associated with significant risks of ischaemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, or venous thromboembolism.
No serious ocular adverse events were noted in both groups.
Systemic adverse events occurred in 10 of 702 patients (1.4%): 7 in the bevacizumab group (7/503; 1.4%) and 3 in the ranibizumab group (3/199; 1.5%).
This difference was not statistically significant (Fisher's exact test, P = 0.573).
In conclusion, compared to ranibizumab, bevacizumab may not increase the risk of systemic adverse events in patients receiving intravitreal injections.
There were no severe local adverse reactions or systemic adverse events.
CONCLUSION: Intravitreal bevacizumab and ranibizumab have equivalent effects on BCVA and CRT and appeare safe over the short-term.
The proportion of patients with serious adverse events was 12.6% in the bevacizumab group and 12.1% in the ranibizumab group (P = 0.88).
The proportion of patients with serious systemic or ocular adverse events was similar in both groups.
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab was noninferior to ranibizumab for visual acuity at 1 year with similar safety profiles.
The number of serious adverse events was similar between the groups over the course of the study.
There was no significant difference in the number of serious adverse events between the treatment groups.

Bevacizumab groups had a statistically higher observed risk of serious adverse events; however, scrutiny of the online supplements shows similar numbers of cardiac and neurologic events in bevacizumab and ranibizumab users.
A difference in cardiovascular safety between the 2 drugs was not apparent on inspection of the supplementary safety data.
Furthermore, there were no significant differences in the decrease of retinal thickness, change of lesion size and number of adverse events between the groups.
CONCLUSIONS: Bevacizumab was equivalent to ranibizumab for visual acuity at all time points over 1 year.
There was no significant difference of decrease of retinal thickness or number of adverse events.
No serious complications were detected in any of the groups.

Conclusion. Both of the bevacizumab and ranibizumab found to be effective in the treatment of nAMD in regards of functional and anatomical outcomes with similar number of treatments and similar side effects.
There were fewer arteriothrombotic events in the bevacizumab group (1.4%) than in the ranibizumab group (4.5%) (P = 0.050) and significantly more cardiac events in the ranibizumab group (P = 0.036).
The numbers of serious adverse events were small.

claim: In myopic children aged 6 to 10 years, orthokeratology lenses slowed axial elongation compared with single-vision glasses over 2 years.
support_summary: Multiple studies consistently report that orthokeratology slows axial elongation relative to spectacle correction in myopic children. The closest and strongest direct evidence comes from randomized or pooled 2-year comparisons showing significantly less axial elongation with orthokeratology than with single-vision spectacles, including high-myopia children aged 8-11 years (0.19 vs 0.51 mm; PMID: 23645372), pooled prospective 2-year data in children (0.41 vs 0.65 mm; PMID: 38906728), and a 2-year comparative study in myopic children where all ortho-k groups had slower axial growth than spectacles (PMID: 31812507). Additional supportive but less exact evidence comes from studies with partial mismatches in duration, comparator, or age range, such as 18-month randomized data in children aged 6-12 years versus single-vision spectacles (PMID: 34233094), 1-year studies versus spectacles or single-vision contact lenses (PMID: 37985346, PMID: 38239610, PMID: 39742589, PMID: 37805535), and mechanistic/observational studies reporting less axial elongation in ortho-k users (PMID: 34866209, PMID: 35690754). One SUPPORT item appears only indirectly relevant because it is a narrative review without direct 2-year comparative data (PMID: 37092614), and another focuses mainly on comparing two ortho-k designs rather than clearly reporting the spectacle comparison in the abstract despite including a spectacle control (PMID: 35995721). Overall, the abstract-level evidence strongly supports the claim, though not every study exactly matches the 6-10-year age window and 2-year timeframe.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This is driven by several direct 2-year studies and pooled analyses showing significantly less axial elongation with orthokeratology than with single-vision spectacles in myopic children, with only minor limitations from some studies having broader age ranges or shorter follow-up.

support_highlights.snippet: The mean ± SD increases in axial length were 0.19 ± 0.21 mm in the PR ortho-k group and 0.51 ± 0.32 mm in the control group (95% confidence interval, -0.55 to -0.12; unpaired t test, p = 0.005).
This single-masked randomized study showed that PR ortho-k effectively slowed myopic progression in high myopes.
Axial length elongation was 63% slower in PR ortho-k-treated children compared with children wearing spectacles.
AL growth was slower in all OrthoK groups than in the control group (all P < 0.05).
Different OrthoK lenses differ minimally in slowing axial elongation effectively in myopic children during 2-years lens wear.

Statistically significant differences in the change in axial length from baseline were found over time, between groups and for the time*group interaction (all p < 0.001), indicating that the rate of increase in axial length over time was significantly lower in the orthokeratology versus the control group.

The lower axial elongation of the orthokeratology versus the control group was statistically significant at all time points (all p < 0.001), with significant differences being also present between each of the different pairs of time points (all p < 0.001).

The change in axial length at the 2-year visit in comparison to baseline for the orthokeratology and control groups were 0.41 ± 0.25 and 0.65 ± 0.30 mm, respectively, thus providing a treatment effect following 2-years of lens wear of 0.24 mm (95 % confidence intervals: 0.15 to 0.34 mm).
Orthokeratology lens wear slows the axial elongation of the eye in comparison to spectacle lens wear in myopic children.
The average AL elongation in the OKL group was 0.24 mm smaller as compared to the SVS group (95% confidence interval 0.12-0.36, mixed model adjusted for baseline sex, age and AL).
Orthokeratology lenses reduced AL elongation in myopic Scandinavian children by 59%, with no treatment-requiring or vision-threatening adverse events.
In the OK group, multivariate regression analysis showed changes in accommodative lag area were associated with AL progression in the first 6 months but not in the 1-year analysis.
A significant positive correlation between improved accommodative accuracy and slowed axial elongation was only observed during the first 6 months of treatment.
Less axial elongation (AE) occurred in the OK group (P = 0.021).
Long-term OK treatment slowed myopia progression by reshaping the cornea.
Smaller ARTh after OK lens wear indicated a better refractive state and slower AE and could predict OK lens treatment outcomes.
To investigate the effectiveness of orthokeratology (ortho-k) lenses and corneal changes with increased compression factor for myopia control over a 2-year period.
The myopia control effectiveness was determined by axial elongation.

Considering ortho-k groups only, the mean axial elongation of participants wearing ortho-k lenses of conventional compression factor (0.75 D) and increased compression factor (1.75 D) were 0.53 ± 0.29 and 0.35 ± 0.29 mm, respectively, over the 2-year study period.
Participants wearing ortho-k lenses of increased compression factor further slowed axial elongation by 34%, when compared with the conventional compression factor without compromising corneal health.
Low dose atropine and Orthokeratology contact lenses appear to be most effective in slowing down myopia progression.
The 5.0 mm-BOZD OK, 6.2 mm-BOZD OK, and DISC groups exhibited significantly slower AL elongation than the SCL group.

Overall, 5.0 mm-BOZD OK lenses, 6.2 mm-BOZD OK lenses, and DISC lenses were effective in retarding AL elongation in 8-11-year-old myopes compared with single-vision SCLs, but for children with SER less than - 2.50 D, fitting with 5.0 mm-BOZD OK lenses and DISC lenses yielded better myopia control efficacy compared to wearing single-vision SCLs or 6.2 mm-BOZD OK lenses.
The annual axial elongation was 0.23 ± 0.19 mm, 0.22 ± 0.20 mm, and 0.39 ± 0.27 mm in the ortho-k, atropine, and spectacle groups, respectively, with significant difference (F = 23.251, P = 0.000).
The axial length was delayed to increase by 41.03% and 43.59% within a year in patients with ortho-k and atropine, respectively, as compared to patients with spectacles (F = 0.006, P = 0.936).
Ortho-k lenses and 0.01% atropine can achieve similar efficacy of myopia retardation, which was significantly better than that obtained with single-vision spectacles, in myopic children.

This study aims to assess and compare the impact of Orthokeratology Double Reservoir Lens (DRL) versus Single Vision Lenses (SVL) on axial elongation and anterior chamber biometric parameters in myopic children over a 6- and 12-month treatment period in France.
Orthokeratology resulted in an 86 and 70% reduction in axial elongation after 6 and 12 months of lens wear, respectively, compared to the single-vision spectacles group.
After 12 months of treatment, the OK group showed a reduced rate of AL elongation.

claim: In myopic children aged 6 to 10 years, younger age was associated with faster axial elongation and greater risk of rapid myopic progression than older age, regardless of orthokeratology treatment or single-vision glasses.
support_summary: Multiple studies consistently report that younger baseline age is associated with faster myopia progression and/or greater axial elongation in children. The strongest directly relevant evidence comes from COMET, where younger baseline age was independently associated with faster 3-year myopic progression and greater axial elongation after adjustment for treatment, with children aged 6-7 years progressing fastest (PMID: 16009841). Several orthokeratology-focused studies also found younger age predicted faster axial elongation during ortho-k wear (PMID: 30482510, PMID: 35135507, PMID: 37173387), and one study comparing ortho-k with single-vision spectacles found baseline age was negatively associated with axial elongation in both groups, which is especially aligned with the claim’s “regardless of treatment” wording (PMID: 30687783). Additional supportive but less direct evidence shows axial elongation and myopia progression are greatest at younger ages in broader pediatric or mixed-age cohorts (PMID: 29923883, PMID: 32334434, PMID: 35511119, PMID: 39373064); one item placed in the contradict group actually supports the claim by showing negative age correlation and more rapid progression in younger versus older children in both ortho-k and control groups (PMID: 27464993).
contradict_summary: There is little true contradiction from the abstracts. Most so-called contradicting studies either actually support the claim’s age effect or are treatment-context mismatches: older age being associated with less progression under atropine (PMID: 39122352) and greater axial length shortening with photobiomodulation in older children (PMID: 38664659) are not direct tests of the claim about ortho-k versus single-vision glasses. A study of ortho-k found that each additional year of age changed the probability of faster axial elongation, but the abstract wording is internally inconsistent and does not clearly contradict the claim; its conclusion that axial length did not change significantly in children older than 11 years is more compatible with younger children progressing faster (PMID: 31694069). Another reanalysis emphasizes that younger control subjects had the greatest and most rapid axial elongation while ortho-k reduced rapid progression risk in younger children, which challenges only an over-strong reading of “regardless of treatment” if interpreted as equal risk across treatments, not the age association itself (PMID: 28253404). Overall, the contradiction set contains substantial categorization noise, with at least one clearly misgrouped supportive study (PMID: 27464993).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Evidence repeatedly shows that younger myopic children have faster axial elongation and greater progression risk, and one particularly relevant study found age predicted axial elongation in both orthokeratology and single-vision spectacle groups, while most purported contradictions are either miscategorized or concern different treatments.

claim: In myopic children aged 6 to 10 years, orthokeratology treatment caused some discontinuations because of adverse events.
support_summary: One comparative 2-year pediatric study found that orthokeratology (OK) in children aged 6-12 years was associated with adverse events, with 9 OK subjects experiencing 16 events, and noted that 11 events were solely attributable to OK wear; however, the abstract also states that discontinuations were significantly more frequent in the spectacle group than in the OK group, so it does not clearly show OK discontinuations caused by adverse events (PMID: 22773180). A pooled safety analysis of three prospective studies in myopic children reported 28 adverse events among 125 orthokeratology subjects, including 6 significant events, but the abstract does not state that these events caused discontinuations, making this only indirect support at best (PMID: 39003152). Overall, the supporting literature mainly establishes that adverse events occur during pediatric orthokeratology, but provides limited direct abstract-level evidence that adverse events specifically led to discontinuation in the target age range. There is also a discrepancy in that the strongest “supporting” study actually emphasizes fewer discontinuations with orthokeratology than with spectacles.
contradict_summary: Several studies describe pediatric orthokeratology as having no serious or treatment-requiring adverse events, which weighs against the claim that adverse events caused discontinuations. A 6-month pilot in children aged 10-16 years reported no serious adverse events (PMID: 17478340), a 1-month toric orthokeratology study in children aged 6-12 years found no significant lens binding, corneal staining, or other adverse events (PMID: 22561203), and an 18-month randomized study in children aged 6-12 years reported no treatment-requiring or vision-threatening adverse events (PMID: 34233094). However, these abstracts generally do not directly report discontinuation outcomes, so they contradict the claim mainly by suggesting a favorable safety profile rather than explicitly proving no adverse-event-related discontinuations. One cited contradiction study is less directly relevant because it concerns soft contact lenses rather than orthokeratology, though it reported that adverse events never caused permanent discontinuation in 7- to 11-year-olds (PMID: 35413027).
conclusion: The statement is overall more contradicted than supported. The best-matched evidence shows pediatric orthokeratology does produce some adverse events, but the abstracts do not clearly document that these caused discontinuations, while multiple studies report no serious or treatment-requiring adverse events and one key comparative study even found fewer discontinuations with orthokeratology than spectacles.

support_highlights.snippet: A higher incidence of adverse events was found with OK compared with SV (p < 0.001).
Nine OK subjects experienced 16 adverse events (7 significant and 9 non-significant).
Most adverse events were found between 6 and 12 months of lens wear, with 11 solely attributable to OK wear.
Significantly more discontinuations were found with SV in comparison with OK (p < 0.05).
Of these, 101 (81 %) and 88 (75 %) orthokeratology and control subjects completed the 2-year follow-up period, respectively.

Nineteen orthokeratology subjects experienced 28 adverse events, of which 6 were significant, whereas just one adverse event was found in the control group; this difference was statistically significant (p < 0.001).
Most adverse events found in the orthokeratology group were corneal in nature, primarily corneal abrasion/staining, accounting for around 40 % of all adverse events.

Of the 28 adverse events, only 18 (3 significant) are likely to be contact lens-related, leading to incidence rates of total and device-related adverse events per 100 patient years of lens wear (95 % confidence intervals) of 13.1 (9.2-18.2) and 8.4 (5.4-10.7), respectively.

contradict_highlights.snippet: No serious adverse events occurred during the study.
No significant lens binding, corneal staining, or other adverse events were observed during this period of lens wear.
No treatment-requiring or vision-threatening adverse events were observed.
Orthokeratology lenses reduced AL elongation in myopic Scandinavian children by 59%, with no treatment-requiring or vision-threatening adverse events.
None of the ocular adverse events were serious or severe or caused permanent contact lens discontinuation.

The adverse events experienced by 7- to 11-year-old myopic children rarely required meaningful treatment and never led to permanent discontinuation of contact lens wear or loss of best-corrected vision.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept given monthly or every 2 months after three initial monthly doses achieved vision outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Direct support comes from analyses of the phase 3 VIEW trials showing that aflibercept 2 mg every 8 weeks after 3 monthly doses produced visual-function outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab over 52 weeks, with similar NEI VFQ-25 changes across subscales (PMID: 25439429). A subgroup analysis of VIEW in Asian patients also found no significant differences in best-corrected visual acuity gains between aflibercept 2q4 or 2q8 and monthly ranibizumab through Week 96, although aflibercept showed greater anatomic drying in some measures (PMID: 29280937). Additional, less direct support includes a review summarizing phase III trials as showing comparable vision gains for aflibercept every 4 or 8 weeks versus monthly ranibizumab (PMID: 22973088), and smaller comparative studies reporting similar 12-month visual acuity outcomes between aflibercept and ranibizumab but using less exact regimens or limited sample sizes (PMID: 26166797, PMID: 27230919). One included paper is a cost-effectiveness analysis that assumes, rather than independently demonstrates, similar efficacy based on VIEW data, so it is only indirect support for the claim (PMID: 29952707).
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. The strongest evidence comes from large randomized VIEW trial analyses directly showing similar visual outcomes for aflibercept monthly or every 8 weeks after three loading doses versus monthly ranibizumab, while the remaining studies are broadly consistent but more indirect or lower quality.

support_highlights.snippet: This analysis included patients who received intravitreal aflibercept 2.0 mg every 8 weeks (2q8; n=607) or ranibizumab 0.5 mg every 4 weeks (0.5q4; n=595).

Patients were randomized 1:1:1:1 to receive intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 (after 3 initial monthly doses), intravitreal aflibercept 2q4, intravitreal aflibercept 0.5q4, or ranibizumab 0.5q4 in the study eye.

Mean change from baseline to 52 weeks was similar for ranibizumab 0.5q4 and intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 across all 12 subscales, with the greatest improvements noted for mental health and general vision (9.0-11.6 points, both treatments, both studies).

Mean change from baseline to 52 weeks in NEI VFQ-25 composite score (pooled data) stratified by clinical response showed meaningful improvement only in patients who gained 5 Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy letters or more (7.3 and 7.8 points for intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 and ranibizumab 0.5q4, respectively).

Visual function outcomes were similar across all NEI VFQ-25 subscales over 52 weeks for intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 and ranibizumab 0.5q4, with clinically meaningful improvement recorded in 6 of 12 subscales.

We evaluated data from VIEW 1 and VIEW 2, comparing functional and morphologic outcomes at Week 96 between intravitreal aflibercept 2 mg monthly (2q4) or 2 mg bimonthly after 3 initial monthly doses (2q8) versus ranibizumab 0.5 mg monthly among Asian patients (n = 269) and between Asian and white patients (n = 2044).

In Asian patients, there were no significant differences between intravitreal aflibercept 2q4 and 2q8 compared with ranibizumab in mean gain in best-corrected visual acuity (10.23 and 8.35 vs. 8.51 letters).

In Asian patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, functional and morphologic outcomes were largely similar between intravitreal aflibercept and ranibizumab groups, and to results seen in white patients.

Given the similar efficacy and safety profiles between IAI and ranibizumab, their associated costs and comparative cost-effectiveness are key factors in determining which one represents a more rational investment of scarce health care resources to help address the increasing cost of prescription drugs in the United States, a source of concern for patients, prescribers, payers, and policymakers.
Over a lifetime, IAI 2q8 provided equal health benefits with Rq4 (5.44 QALYs) at a lower total cost ($33,745 vs. $48,031) as a result of fewer injections.
Phase III trials showed that monthly or every other month injections of aflibercept prevent vision loss (fewer than 15 letters) in 95% of patients.
Additionally, aflibercept injections every 4 or 8 weeks produce average vision gains of 6.9 letters to 10.9 letters, comparable with those achieved with monthly ranibizumab.
Outcomes were similar in the 2 groups over a 12-month study duration, with no statistical difference.

This comparative pilot study suggests that neovascular age-related macular degeneration requiring monthly retreatment with ranibizumab may respond in similar ways to both ranibizumab and aflibercept treatment.
Patients initially received three monthly injections, and were re-treated when neovascularization recurred.
There was no difference in BCVA between the two groups at either diagnosis (P = 0.560) or 12 months (P = 0.702).
The 12-month treatment outcome of intravitreal ranibizumab was similar to that of intravitreal aflibercept, with a comparable injection frequency.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept produced similar improvements in visual acuity and anatomic retinal outcomes as monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Several comparative studies reported broadly similar visual outcomes between aflibercept and ranibizumab in neovascular AMD. A pilot randomized comparison in patients needing monthly ranibizumab found no statistical difference in outcomes over 12 months after switching to aflibercept versus continuing ranibizumab (PMID: 26166797), and a retrospective 12-month study in treatment-naive eyes likewise found no difference in BCVA at baseline or 12 months (PMID: 27230919). In eyes with retinal pigment epithelial detachment, no significant between-group difference was seen in visual acuity change or RPED dimensions, with ~30% complete RPED resolution in each group (PMID: 28820848). VIEW subgroup analyses in Asian patients showed no significant difference in BCVA gain between aflibercept and monthly ranibizumab, although some morphologic measures favored aflibercept, creating a minor within-study discrepancy with a strict “similar anatomic outcomes” interpretation (PMID: 29280937); similarly, a real-world post-bevacizumab switch study found comparable BCVA and CMT outcomes between ranibizumab and aflibercept despite fewer injections with aflibercept (PMID: 39431040).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is dominated by switch studies in ranibizumab-insufficient responders, a population not fully matching the original claim, but several abstracts suggest outcomes are not always similar. Some studies reported aflibercept produced notable anatomic improvement without significant visual acuity gain after prior ranibizumab failure (PMID: 33567025, PMID: 24895562, PMID: 25461263, PMID: 27652915, PMID: 28393034), while others suggested aflibercept had stronger effects on certain anatomic measures such as PED or choroidal thickness than ranibizumab (PMID: 25395483, PMID: 27115852), implying non-equivalence rather than similarity. A more direct comparative study in occult CNV found greater BCVA improvement with ranibizumab at 3 months, even though aflibercept reduced some fluid-related measures faster (PMID: 26604674). Another case series described worsening subretinal fluid after switching well-controlled monthly ranibizumab eyes to aflibercept, with improvement after switching back to ranibizumab (PMID: 27354759). Some contradiction-group articles are only indirectly relevant because they lack head-to-head comparison and mainly show aflibercept benefit in refractory disease rather than true contradiction of equivalence (for example PMID: 23549101, PMID: 24362854).
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported, because multiple head-to-head or comparative studies directly report similar visual acuity outcomes and broadly comparable functional/anatomic results between aflibercept and ranibizumab. However, the evidence is not uniform: several refractory-disease switch studies and at least one comparative study indicate subgroup-specific differences, especially in anatomic response and occasional superiority of one agent over the other.

claim: In patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, intravitreal aflibercept had a safety profile similar to monthly ranibizumab.
support_summary: Direct supportive evidence comes mainly from a VIEW trials subgroup analysis in Asian patients, which reported that serious treatment-emergent ocular adverse events occurred in fewer than 8% of treated eyes and were evenly distributed across aflibercept and ranibizumab subgroups, consistent with similar safety profiles (PMID: 29280937). A review of aflibercept for neovascular AMD states that randomized trials showed an excellent safety profile for anti-VEGF therapy and that aflibercept every 8 weeks after loading was clinically equivalent and noninferior to monthly ranibizumab, but the abstract does not provide explicit head-to-head safety event data (PMID: 23413903). Other cited studies mostly address efficacy or treatment response rather than safety: a small randomized pilot found similar outcomes after switching to aflibercept versus continuing ranibizumab but did not report safety details (PMID: 26166797); a noncomparative conversion study reported no significant adverse event with aflibercept alone, without direct comparison to ranibizumab (PMID: 26398691); and a retrospective comparative study found similar visual outcomes and injection frequency but no explicit safety comparison (PMID: 27230919). Overall, the supporting set contains one reasonably direct safety comparison and several only indirectly relevant studies, creating some evidence-quality discrepancy within the support group.
contradict_summary: The main contradicting study is a small retrospective case series describing 17 eyes with neovascular AMD that were previously well controlled on monthly ranibizumab but developed increased subretinal fluid after switching to aflibercept; fluid resolved after switching back to ranibizumab (PMID: 27354759). However, this abstract primarily reports worsening anatomic response in a selected subset rather than direct comparative safety adverse-event rates, and it also states that no eye had persistent decline in visual acuity. Thus, it suggests that aflibercept may perform worse in some patients but is only an indirect contradiction of the claim about overall safety profile similarity.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest abstract-level evidence is the VIEW subgroup analysis showing evenly distributed serious ocular adverse events between aflibercept and ranibizumab (PMID: 29280937), whereas the contradictory evidence is limited to a small selected case series focused more on anatomic worsening after switching than on overall safety.

support_highlights.snippet: Serious treatment-emergent ocular adverse events occurred in <8% of treated eyes, evenly distributed across subgroups.
Randomized trials have demonstrated the excellent safety profile and significant benefit of anti-VEGF therapy in maintaining vision.

Having received US FDA approval for neovascular AMD in November 2011, aflibercept given every 8 weeks after a loading dose was 'clinically equivalent' and statistically noninferior to the current FDA-approved therapy ranibizumab (Lucentis(®); Genentech, CA, USA), given every 4 weeks.
No significant adverse event was observed during the follow-up.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Herein, we identify a unique subset of patients whose eyes with neovascular AMD were previously well controlled with ranibizumab injections were then worsened after being switched to aflibercept.
All eyes developed increased subretinal fluid when switched from ranibizumab to aflibercept.
Fourteen patients were switched back to ranibizumab after a single injection of aflibercept and had subsequent rapid resolution of subretinal fluid.

Three patients continued with monthly aflibercept injections for two subsequent months and demonstrated the persistence of the increased subretinal fluid until they were switched back to treatment with ranibizumab at which time the fluid resolved.

Switching from intravitreal ranibizumab to aflibercept in eyes with well-controlled neovascular AMD may result in worsening in a subset of patients and resolves when therapy is switched back to ranibizumab.

claim: In high myopic children aged 8 to 11 years, partial reduction orthokeratology with daytime spectacles for residual refractive error slowed myopic progression compared with full correction using single-vision spectacles.
support_summary: One retrospective 2-year study in Chinese children found that orthokeratology was associated with substantially less axial length elongation than spectacle wear overall, with about a 51% reduction across the cohort (PMID: 25417926). The abstract also reports benefit within the high-myopia subgroup during the first year, and concludes ortho-k is effective in reducing myopia progression in children with low, moderate, and high myopia (PMID: 25417926). However, this study does not specifically describe partial-reduction orthokeratology combined with daytime spectacles for residual refractive error, nor is the age range limited to 8-11 years in the abstract, so it is only indirect support for the precise claim. No clear discrepancy between abstract findings and its supportive categorization is evident, but applicability to the exact intervention is limited.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
conclusion: The statement is overall somewhat supported, but only indirectly. The available evidence suggests orthokeratology slows axial elongation versus spectacles, including in highly myopic children, yet it does not directly test partial-reduction orthokeratology with daytime spectacle correction in 8- to 11-year-old high myopes.

support_highlights.snippet: The control group exhibited more changes in axial length than the ortho-k group at both 12 months (0.39 ± 0.21 mm vs 0.16 ± 0.17 mm, p <0.001) and 24 months (0.70 ± 0.35 mm vs 0.34 ± 0.29 mm, p <0.001).
Axial length elongation was estimated to be slower by about 51% in the ortho-k group.
Similar results were found for the subgroups (49%, 59% and 46% reductions, respectively).
In the high myopia groups, significant differences were only found between the ortho-k and control groups during the first year (0.16 ± 0.18 mm vs 0.34 ± 0.22 mm, p = 0.004).
This 2-year study indicates that ortho-k contact lens wear is effective for reducing myopia progression in children with low, moderate and high myopia.

claim: In high myopic children aged 8 to 11 years, partial reduction orthokeratology with daytime spectacles for residual refractive error reduced axial length elongation compared with full correction using single-vision spectacles.
support_summary: One prospective clinical study in high-myopia children found that both partial-reduction orthokeratology regimens produced significantly less axial elongation over 12 months than single-vision spectacle correction, with control eyes showing axial length increase of 0.294±0.136 mm versus 0.101±0.176 mm and 0.123±0.193 mm in the ortho-k groups (PMID: 31361656). The same study concluded that both ortho-k regimens had similar effects in controlling increases in axial length and refractive error in high-myopia children compared with spectacles, which is directionally consistent with the claim (PMID: 31361656). However, the abstract does not clearly state the specific age range of 8 to 11 years or explicitly describe daytime spectacles for residual refractive error, so support is indirect rather than exact on all claim details.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available study shows reduced axial length elongation with partial-reduction orthokeratology versus single-vision spectacles in high-myopia children (PMID: 31361656). Confidence is moderate rather than strong because the evidence is based on a single abstract and does not fully confirm all specific claim details, especially the exact 8-11 year age range and residual daytime spectacle use.

support_highlights.snippet: Subjects in the control group had a significant increase in refractive error (0.565±0.313 D) and axial length (0.294±0.136 mm), when compared with subjects in the ortho-k-treated groups (P<0.05).
However, there were no significant differences in changes in refractive error and axial length between ortho-k groups 1 (0.101±0.176 mm) and 2 (0.123±0.193 mm) at 12 months (P>0.05).
The two ortho-k regimens, target reduction of 6.00 D and target of 4.00 D, had similar effects in controlling the increase in axial length and refractive error in high-myopia children.

claim: In high myopic children aged 8 to 11 years, partial reduction orthokeratology with daytime spectacles for residual refractive error resulted in less increase in myopia over follow-up than full correction using single-vision spectacles.
support_summary: One prospective clinical study in high-myopia children reported that both partial-reduction orthokeratology regimens produced significantly less increase in refractive error and axial length over 12 months than single-vision spectacles, supporting the general direction of the claim (PMID: 31361656). The abstract specifically states that the control spectacle group had greater refractive error and axial length increases, while the orthokeratology groups had smaller axial elongation (PMID: 31361656). However, the abstract does not clearly confirm the claim’s exact age range of 8 to 11 years or explicitly describe daytime spectacle correction for residual refractive error, so support is only partial. No major discrepancy is apparent within the single supporting item, but its population/intervention details are not fully matched to the source claim.
contradict_summary: There are no contradicting evidence items.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, because the available study shows lower myopia progression/axial elongation with partial-reduction orthokeratology than with single-vision spectacles (PMID: 31361656). However, support is moderate rather than strong since the abstract does not fully verify the exact age subgroup or the residual daytime spectacle-correction details in the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: This study aims to compare the increase in refractive error and axial length, variation of endothelium cells, and ratio of corneal staining between two regimens of high myopia-partial reduction orthokeratology (ortho-k) in children.

These subjects were randomly divided into three groups: (1) ortho-k group 1, subjects with a target myopia reduction of 6.00 D; (2) ortho-k group 2, subjects with a target myopia reduction of 4.00 D; and (3) control group, the refractive error of subjects was corrected using a pair of single-vision spectacles.
Subjects in the control group had a significant increase in refractive error (0.565±0.313 D) and axial length (0.294±0.136 mm), when compared with subjects in the ortho-k-treated groups (P<0.05).
The two ortho-k regimens, target reduction of 6.00 D and target of 4.00 D, had similar effects in controlling the increase in axial length and refractive error in high-myopia children.

claim: Intravitreal aflibercept and intravitreal ranibizumab produced similar visual acuity improvement and similar prevention of visual acuity loss in patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration over 96 weeks.
support_summary: Several comparative studies report no significant difference in visual acuity outcomes between intravitreal aflibercept and ranibizumab for neovascular AMD, including a 96-week subgroup analysis of the VIEW trials in Asian patients showing no significant differences in mean BCVA gain versus ranibizumab (PMID: 29280937). Additional retrospective and observational studies at 6, 12, and 36 months similarly found comparable BCVA change or clinical effectiveness between the two drugs (PMID: 27230919, PMID: 29855194, PMID: 34162188, PMID: 28820848, PMID: 25395483). Some support is indirect rather than exact, such as studies focusing on visual function rather than acuity or on switching therapy rather than direct head-to-head long-term comparison (PMID: 25439429, PMID: 26669334, PMID: 29324593). A recurring theme is that anatomic outcomes sometimes differed, with aflibercept showing greater drying or thickness reduction despite similar vision outcomes (PMID: 29280937, PMID: 25395483). Overall, the strongest support comes from the VIEW-derived 96-week data and multiple real-world studies consistently finding similar visual outcomes.
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence is limited and mostly comes from nonparallel or short-term settings rather than direct long-term head-to-head 96-week comparisons. One retrospective switch study found that patients insufficiently responsive to ranibizumab improved after switching to aflibercept, suggesting aflibercept may outperform ranibizumab in a selected resistant population, but this design is not a balanced comparison of treatment-naive groups and therefore only indirectly challenges the claim (PMID: 24362854). Another retrospective 3-month study in occult CNV reported greater BCVA improvement with ranibizumab than aflibercept during the induction phase, indicating possible early subgroup-specific differences (PMID: 26604674). These studies suggest that equivalence may not hold in all subpopulations or phases of treatment, but they do not directly refute the specific 96-week overall claim. Thus, the contradiction is modest and context-specific.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence includes a 96-week VIEW subgroup analysis directly showing no significant difference in BCVA gain between aflibercept and ranibizumab (PMID: 29280937), while the opposing studies are shorter-term or selected-population analyses that do not closely match the claim's overall 96-week comparison.

support_highlights.snippet: There was no difference in BCVA between the two groups at either diagnosis (P = 0.560) or 12 months (P = 0.702).
CONCLUSION: The 12-month treatment outcome of intravitreal ranibizumab was similar to that of intravitreal aflibercept, with a comparable injection frequency.

CONCLUSION: In Asian patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, functional and morphologic outcomes were largely similar between intravitreal aflibercept and ranibizumab groups, and to results seen in white patients.

In addition, BCVA demonstrated an increase of 4.1 ± 0.44 letters in IVR group and 4.36 ± 0.67 letters in IVA group after 36 months compared to the baseline (p > 0.05).

CONCLUSIONS: Visual function outcomes were similar across all NEI VFQ-25 subscales over 52 weeks for intravitreal aflibercept 2q8 and ranibizumab 0.5q4, with clinically meaningful improvement recorded in 6 of 12 subscales.
Mean logMAR visual acuity (VA) in eyes treated with ranibizumab changed by - 0.089 ± 0.310 (mean ± SD; P = 0.0003), which correlates to an approximate gain of 4.5 letters.
Mean logMAR VA in eyes with intraretinal macular fluid treated with aflibercept changed by -0.079 ± 0.134 (mean ± SD; P = 0.006), which correlates to an approximate gain of 4 letters.

CONCLUSION: Eyes with neovascular age-related macular degeneration and RPED showed significant functional and anatomical responses after 6 months of intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor injections.
However, we found no significant difference in anatomical response or change in visual acuity between eyes treated with ranibizumab compared with aflibercept.
CONCLUSION: There was a low rate of retinal pigment epithelium tears after aflibercept injections, similar to ranibizumab.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Mean VA changed from 0.47 logMAR before the first aflibercept injection to 0.25 logMAR after the last aflibercept injection.
Interestingly, 33 % of cases that did not show a functional improvement under ranibizumab therapy gained visual acuity after aflibercept treatment.
CONCLUSION: Aflibercept appears to be an effective choice for patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration who were resistant to previous therapy of ranibizumab.
