claim: Intravitreal aflibercept given every 8 weeks after initial monthly injections achieved visual acuity outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab while requiring fewer injections in patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration over 96 weeks.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the phase 3 VIEW 1/2 trials, where aflibercept 2 mg every 8 weeks after 3 monthly doses was clinically equivalent/noninferior to monthly ranibizumab for maintaining vision and had mean BCVA changes within 0.5 letters of ranibizumab, with similar anatomic outcomes (PMID: 23084240). A 96-week subgroup analysis in Asian patients also found no significant BCVA differences at Week 96 between aflibercept 2q8 and monthly ranibizumab, reinforcing similar functional outcomes over the longer time frame relevant to the claim (PMID: 29280937). A review article summarizes phase III data as showing comparable vision gains with aflibercept every 4 or 8 weeks versus monthly ranibizumab and notes fewer injections in the second year, though this is indirect review-level evidence rather than primary trial reporting (PMID: 22973088). A cost-effectiveness model based on VIEW 1/2 assumes equal health benefits and lower total costs for aflibercept 2q8 because of fewer injections, but this is secondary modeled evidence rather than a direct clinical comparison and should be weighed accordingly (PMID: 29952707).
contradict_summary: Contradictory evidence is limited and mostly indirect to the exact claim. A small retrospective case series reported that 17 eyes previously well controlled on monthly ranibizumab developed increased subretinal fluid after switching to aflibercept, with fluid resolving after switching back; however, this was a selected subgroup, involved switching rather than randomized regimen comparison, and did not show persistent visual acuity decline (PMID: 27354759). A post hoc VIEW analysis found that among eyes with early persistent retinal fluid, monthly aflibercept (2q4) produced greater BCVA gain than both aflibercept 2q8 and monthly ranibizumab, while ranibizumab and aflibercept 2q8 were similar; this suggests 2q8 may be less optimal in that specific subgroup but does not directly contradict similarity between 2q8 and ranibizumab overall (PMID: 27369111). Overall, the contradiction centers on subgroup/anatomic concerns rather than broad evidence that aflibercept 2q8 is inferior to monthly ranibizumab across 96 weeks.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Direct randomized trial evidence and 96-week subgroup data show aflibercept every 8 weeks after loading doses achieves visual outcomes similar to monthly ranibizumab, while the opposing studies are narrower subgroup or switching observations that do not overturn the main trial findings.

support_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: Over a lifetime, IAI 2q8 provided equal health benefits with Rq4 (5.44 QALYs) at a lower total cost ($33,745 vs. $48,031) as a result of fewer injections.
Additionally, aflibercept injections every 4 or 8 weeks produce average vision gains of 6.9 letters to 10.9 letters, comparable with those achieved with monthly ranibizumab.
After one year of regularly administered aflibercept injections, patients required an average of only 4.2 injections during the second year.
Aflibercept promises to decrease the injection frequency required for many patients and appears to serve as an effective "salvage" therapy for patients who respond poorly to other anti-VEGF drugs.

INTERVENTION: Patients were randomized to intravitreal aflibercept 0.5 mg monthly (0.5q4), 2 mg monthly (2q4), 2 mg every 2 months after 3 initial monthly doses (2q8), or ranibizumab 0.5 mg monthly (Rq4).

RESULTS: All aflibercept groups were noninferior and clinically equivalent to monthly ranibizumab for the primary end point (the 2q4, 0.5q4, and 2q8 regimens were 95.1%, 95.9%, and 95.1%, respectively, for VIEW 1, and 95.6%, 96.3%, and 95.6%, respectively, for VIEW 2, whereas monthly ranibizumab was 94.4% in both studies).

In a prespecified integrated analysis of the 2 studies, all aflibercept regimens were within 0.5 letters of the reference ranibizumab for mean change in BCVA; all aflibercept regimens also produced similar improvements in anatomic measures.
CONCLUSIONS: Intravitreal aflibercept dosed monthly or every 2 months after 3 initial monthly doses produced similar efficacy and safety outcomes as monthly ranibizumab.

These studies demonstrate that aflibercept is an effective treatment for AMD, with the every-2-month regimen offering the potential to reduce the risk from monthly intravitreal injections and the burden of monthly monitoring.

METHODS: We evaluated data from VIEW 1 and VIEW 2, comparing functional and morphologic outcomes at Week 96 between intravitreal aflibercept 2 mg monthly (2q4) or 2 mg bimonthly after 3 initial monthly doses (2q8) versus ranibizumab 0.5 mg monthly among Asian patients (n = 269) and between Asian and white patients (n = 2044).

RESULTS: In Asian patients, there were no significant differences between intravitreal aflibercept 2q4 and 2q8 compared with ranibizumab in mean gain in best-corrected visual acuity (10.23 and 8.35 vs. 8.51 letters).

CONCLUSION: In Asian patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration, functional and morphologic outcomes were largely similar between intravitreal aflibercept and ranibizumab groups, and to results seen in white patients.

contradict_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: A total of 17 eyes were included.
All eyes developed increased subretinal fluid when switched from ranibizumab to aflibercept.
Fourteen patients were switched back to ranibizumab after a single injection of aflibercept and had subsequent rapid resolution of subretinal fluid.

Three patients continued with monthly aflibercept injections for two subsequent months and demonstrated the persistence of the increased subretinal fluid until they were switched back to treatment with ranibizumab at which time the fluid resolved.

CONCLUSION: Switching from intravitreal ranibizumab to aflibercept in eyes with well-controlled neovascular AMD may result in worsening in a subset of patients and resolves when therapy is switched back to ranibizumab.
RESULTS: The proportions of eyes with persistent fluid were 29.4%, 18.8%, and 20.3% in the Rq4, 2q4, and 2q8 groups, respectively.
In these eyes, mean BCVA gain from baseline to week 52 was greater with 2q4 compared with Rq4 (P < 0.01) and 2q8 (P < 0.05), whereas it was similar with Rq4 and 2q8 (P = 0.294).
At week 52, similar proportions of eyes gained ≥15 letters (31.5%-35.2%), whereas fewer eyes lost ≥5 letters with 2q4 compared with Rq4 and 2q8 (6.5% vs. 16.6% and 16.2%).
CONCLUSIONS: In patients with early persistent fluid, 2q4 may provide additional clinical benefit over 2q8 or Rq4.

claim: Intravitreal aflibercept and intravitreal ranibizumab had similar safety profiles with respect to arterial thromboembolic events in patients with neovascular age-related macular degeneration over 96 weeks.
support_summary: One large nationwide retrospective cohort study found no significant difference in major arterial thromboembolic events, including ischemic stroke and acute myocardial infarction, between intravitreal ranibizumab and aflibercept over 2 years of follow-up (PMID: 35436315). This generally supports similar systemic safety profiles for these agents with respect to arterial thromboembolic events. However, the study was not limited specifically to neovascular age-related macular degeneration in the abstract, and subgroup analyses suggested lower arterial thromboembolic risk with aflibercept in some populations, which slightly tempers the uniformity of the support (PMID: 35436315).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted because the available comparative cohort study reports no significant overall difference in arterial thromboembolic risk between aflibercept and ranibizumab (PMID: 35436315). Still, support is moderate rather than strong because the abstract does not clearly restrict the population to neovascular age-related macular degeneration and subgroup differences favored aflibercept in certain patients.

support_highlights.snippet: RESULTS: No significant difference was noted between intravitreal ranibizumab and aflibercept injection for arterial thromboembolic risk, including ischemic stroke and acute myocardial infarction, during a 2-year follow-up (adjusted hazard ratio (HR): 0.87, 95% confidence interval (CI): 0.53-1.42; P = .583).
CONCLUSIONS: No significant differences in the risk of major ATEs and glaucoma were found between ranibizumab and aflibercept, and aflibercept might be safe for use in elderly patients.

claim: In participants at risk for advanced age-related macular degeneration who received an AREDS-type supplement, lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation was associated with a lower risk of progression to late age-related macular degeneration compared with no lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from long-term follow-up of AREDS2, in which lutein/zeaxanthin assignment was associated with a modest but statistically significant lower hazard of progression to late AMD versus no lutein/zeaxanthin (HR 0.91, 95% CI 0.84-0.99), with a stronger effect among those assigned beta carotene and in direct comparison versus beta carotene (PMID: 35653117). Two additional supporting items are indirect rather than direct clinical tests of the claim: one review states that clinical, epidemiologic, and experimental data suggest lutein/zeaxanthin may protect against AMD but explicitly says randomized trials are needed (PMID: 17552428), and one cell study reports biologic mechanisms by which lutein/zeaxanthin could reduce AMD risk, including attenuation of photooxidative and inflammatory changes in retinal pigment epithelial cells (PMID: 22732187). Overall, support is driven mainly by the AREDS2 long-term follow-up, while the other articles provide only background plausibility. There is also a discrepancy within the evidence base because the main AREDS2 randomized trial abstract did not find a statistically significant primary benefit, whereas later follow-up did.
contradict_summary: The main contradictory evidence is the original AREDS2 randomized clinical trial, which found that adding lutein/zeaxanthin to the AREDS formulation did not significantly reduce progression to advanced AMD in the primary analysis compared with placebo/no lutein/zeaxanthin (HR 0.90, 98.7% CI 0.76-1.07; P=.12) (PMID: 23644932). The abstract reports similar 5-year Kaplan-Meier progression probabilities between placebo and lutein/zeaxanthin groups and concludes that addition of lutein/zeaxanthin did not further reduce risk in primary analyses (PMID: 23644932). However, this contradiction is tempered by the wording that benefit was not shown in primary analyses rather than evidence of harm, and by later follow-up suggesting a modest benefit.
conclusion: The claim is overall somewhat supported but with important inconsistency. The highest-quality direct randomized trial abstract did not show a statistically significant primary benefit (PMID: 23644932), yet long-term AREDS2 follow-up found a modest significant reduction in progression to late AMD with lutein/zeaxanthin (PMID: 35653117), so the total evidence suggests possible benefit rather than a uniformly established effect.

support_highlights.snippet: The hazard ratio (HR) for progression to late AMD comparing lutein/zeaxanthin with no lutein/zeaxanthin was 0.91 (95% CI, 0.84-0.99; P = .02) and comparing ω-3 fatty acids with no ω-3 fatty acids was 1.01 (95% CI, 0.93-1.09; P = .91).
When the lutein/zeaxanthin main effects analysis was restricted to those randomly assigned to beta carotene, the HR was 0.80 (95% CI, 0.68-0.92; P = .002).
A direct analysis of lutein/zeaxanthin vs beta carotene showed the HR for late AMD was 0.85 (95% CI, 0.73-0.98; P = .02).
When compared with beta carotene, lutein/zeaxanthin had a potential beneficial association with late AMD progression.
Moreover, extensive data from clinical, epidemiological and experimental studies suggest that lutein and zeaxanthin might protect against the development of AMD.
Epidemiologic studies suggest that insufficient dietary lutein and zeaxanthin intake or lower serum zeaxanthin levels are associated with increased risk for AMD.

Incubation of the A2E-containing RPE with lutein or zeaxanthin prior to blue light exposure significantly attenuated the photooxidation-induced inactivation of the proteasome and photooxidation-induced changes in expression of MCP-1, IL-8, and CFH.
Together, these data indicate that lutein or zeaxanthin modulates inflammatory responses in cultured RPE in response to photooxidation.
Similar mechanisms may explain salutary effects of lutein and zeaxanthin in reducing the risk for AMD.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Comparison with placebo in the primary analyses demonstrated no statistically significant reduction in progression to advanced AMD (hazard ratio [HR], 0.90 [98.7% CI, 0.76-1.07]; P = .12 for lutein + zeaxanthin; 0.97 [98.7% CI, 0.82-1.16]; P = .70 for DHA + EPA; 0.89 [98.7% CI, 0.75-1.06]; P = .10 for lutein + zeaxanthin and DHA + EPA).

Kaplan-Meier probabilities of progression to advanced AMD by 5 years were 31% (493 eyes [406 participants]) for placebo, 29% (468 eyes [399 participants]) for lutein + zeaxanthin, 31% (507 eyes [416 participants]) for DHA + EPA, and 30% (472 eyes [387 participants]) for lutein + zeaxanthin and DHA + EPA.
Addition of lutein + zeaxanthin, DHA + EPA, or both to the AREDS formulation in primary analyses did not further reduce risk of progression to advanced AMD.

claim: In participants at risk for advanced age-related macular degeneration who received an AREDS-type supplement, lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation was associated with a lower risk of late age-related macular degeneration, including neovascular age-related macular degeneration, compared with beta carotene supplementation.
support_summary: One supporting item provides only indirect support: a comparative topic-modeling analysis of broad macular degeneration nutrition literature identified zeaxanthin among compounds associated with reports of significant benefit and noted follow-up literature support for its effectiveness (PMID: 38660183). However, this article is a methodological/text-mining study rather than a direct clinical comparison, and its abstract does not specifically address AREDS-type supplements, populations at risk for advanced AMD, or a head-to-head comparison of lutein/zeaxanthin versus beta carotene. Thus, the available support is limited and indirect.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is only weakly supported by the provided evidence. The sole cited article suggests zeaxanthin may be linked to beneficial macular degeneration outcomes, but it does not directly test or report the specific comparison of lutein/zeaxanthin versus beta carotene in AREDS-type supplementation for late AMD risk.

support_highlights.snippet: Six compounds were identified as having a particular association with reports of significant results for benefiting MD.
Four of these were further supported in terms of effectiveness upon conducting a follow-up literature search for validation (omega-3 fatty acids, copper, zeaxanthin, and nitrates).

claim: In participants with bilateral large drusen at baseline who received an AREDS-type supplement, lutein/zeaxanthin supplementation was associated with a lower risk of progression to late age-related macular degeneration and neovascular age-related macular degeneration compared with beta carotene supplementation, with no clear difference for central geographic atrophy.
support_summary: One long-term AREDS2 follow-up study supports part of the claim by reporting that direct comparison of lutein/zeaxanthin versus beta carotene was associated with a lower risk of progression to late AMD overall (HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.73-0.98) and concluded that lutein/zeaxanthin was an appropriate replacement for beta carotene (PMID: 35653117). However, the abstract does not specifically report outcomes restricted to the bilateral large drusen subgroup, nor does it provide separate abstract-level results for neovascular AMD or central geographic atrophy, so support for those finer claim elements is indirect rather than explicit. Overall, the supporting evidence is therefore limited but directionally consistent with the claim.
contradict_summary: The main AREDS2 randomized trial enrolled participants at risk for progression, including those with bilateral large drusen, but its primary abstract-level analysis found no statistically significant reduction in advanced AMD from adding lutein/zeaxanthin versus placebo within the AREDS formulation (HR 0.90, 98.7% CI 0.76-1.07; P=.12) (PMID: 23644932). The same abstract also states there was no apparent effect of beta carotene elimination on progression to advanced AMD, which weighs against a clear advantage of lutein/zeaxanthin over beta carotene at the trial’s primary-analysis level (PMID: 23644932). However, this is not a perfect contradiction to the claim because the abstract does not present the specific head-to-head subgroup comparison in participants with bilateral large drusen receiving an AREDS-type supplement, nor separate neovascular AMD or central geographic atrophy results. Thus, the contradiction is based more on lack of significant primary-trial benefit than on a direct refutation of every claim component.
conclusion: The evidence is mixed but leans slightly toward support. A long-term AREDS2 follow-up provides direct abstract-level evidence that lutein/zeaxanthin performed better than beta carotene for late AMD overall, but the original AREDS2 trial abstract did not show a significant primary benefit and did not clearly confirm the claim’s specific subgroup and lesion-type details.

support_highlights.snippet: The hazard ratio (HR) for progression to late AMD comparing lutein/zeaxanthin with no lutein/zeaxanthin was 0.91 (95% CI, 0.84-0.99; P = .02) and comparing ω-3 fatty acids with no ω-3 fatty acids was 1.01 (95% CI, 0.93-1.09; P = .91).
When the lutein/zeaxanthin main effects analysis was restricted to those randomly assigned to beta carotene, the HR was 0.80 (95% CI, 0.68-0.92; P = .002).
A direct analysis of lutein/zeaxanthin vs beta carotene showed the HR for late AMD was 0.85 (95% CI, 0.73-0.98; P = .02).
When compared with beta carotene, lutein/zeaxanthin had a potential beneficial association with late AMD progression.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Comparison with placebo in the primary analyses demonstrated no statistically significant reduction in progression to advanced AMD (hazard ratio [HR], 0.90 [98.7% CI, 0.76-1.07]; P = .12 for lutein + zeaxanthin; 0.97 [98.7% CI, 0.82-1.16]; P = .70 for DHA + EPA; 0.89 [98.7% CI, 0.75-1.06]; P = .10 for lutein + zeaxanthin and DHA + EPA).
There was no apparent effect of beta carotene elimination or lower-dose zinc on progression to advanced AMD.
Addition of lutein + zeaxanthin, DHA + EPA, or both to the AREDS formulation in primary analyses did not further reduce risk of progression to advanced AMD.

claim: In people with age-related macular degeneration, risk of progression to advanced age-related macular degeneration increased with older age and greater baseline drusen severity.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from a large prospective AREDS analysis showing that both age and drusen size in both eyes were independently associated with progression to advanced AMD over 12 years (PMID: 21959373). Additional studies support the drusen-severity component by showing that higher baseline AMD/drusen severity scales predict markedly higher 5-year incidence of advanced or late AMD (PMID: 19752423, PMID: 23332590). Several other cited articles support only parts of the claim, mainly that older age is associated with more severe AMD or higher AMD risk, but they do not directly evaluate progression from existing AMD with baseline drusen severity (PMID: 19027484, PMID: 23828466, PMID: 2786410, PMID: 7862408). Some SUPPORT items are therefore only indirectly relevant or somewhat miscategorized as direct support because they address prevalence/risk of AMD features rather than progression to advanced AMD specifically (PMID: 2786408, PMID: 7786213, PMID: 7862408).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. This is driven mainly by strong direct prospective evidence that older age and greater baseline drusen burden independently predict progression to advanced AMD, while the remaining studies provide mostly indirect or partial corroboration.

claim: In people with age-related macular degeneration, women and current smokers had a higher risk of developing neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: Support is limited and mainly pertains to smoking rather than sex differences. A population-based epidemiological study found that smoking was associated with a higher risk of atrophic maculopathy, but not exudative/neovascular maculopathy; in the same abstract, exudative maculopathy was instead linked to hypnotic use, so this is only indirect support for the smoking part of the claim and not for women or neovascular AMD specifically (PMID: 1557977). A review article states that cigarette smoking is among the most consistent risk factors for age-related macular degeneration overall, but it does not report women being at higher risk or specifically tie smoking to incident neovascular AMD in the abstract (PMID: 19027484). Overall, the supporting set is broad or indirect and does not directly establish that women and current smokers are at higher risk of neovascular AMD.
contradict_summary: One large population-based study directly reported no sex differences in atrophic or neovascular age-related macular degeneration, which contradicts the claim that women have higher risk of neovascular AMD (PMID: 7862408). The same abstract shows strong age-related increases in neovascular AMD prevalence but provides no smoking data, so the contradiction is specific to the sex component rather than the smoking component. This makes the opposing evidence more directly relevant to the women-related part of the claim than the supporting evidence is.
conclusion: The statement is overall unclear to somewhat contradicted. The available support only indirectly links smoking to AMD generally, whereas the best direct evidence in this set specifically reports no sex difference for neovascular AMD, undermining the claim that women are at higher risk.

claim: In people with age-related macular degeneration who developed advanced disease, visual acuity declined substantially, whereas eyes with large drusen that never progressed to advanced disease generally retained good visual acuity.
support_summary: One study directly supports the first half of the claim by showing that eyes with drusenoid pigment epithelial detachment, a high-risk intermediate AMD feature, had high rates of both progression to late AMD and substantial vision loss: 67% progressed to late AMD within 5 years and 46% lost at least 3 lines of visual acuity (PMID: 30142373). A second article is mainly a classification consensus statement rather than an outcomes study, but it supports the broader context that large drusen represent intermediate AMD with much higher risk of progression to late AMD than smaller drusen (PMID: 23332590). However, neither abstract directly demonstrates that eyes with large drusen that never progress generally retain good visual acuity, so support for that part of the claim is indirect. There is also a relevance discrepancy in PMID: 23332590 because it addresses risk stratification more than visual acuity outcomes.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted, but only moderately. The evidence clearly supports substantial visual acuity decline in eyes that progress toward late/advanced AMD, whereas the claim that nonprogressing large-drusen eyes generally retain good visual acuity is only weakly and indirectly addressed by the provided abstracts.

support_highlights.snippet: Drusenoid pigment epithelial detachment was associated with increased risk of ≥3 lines of VA loss (HR, 3.08; CI, 2.41-3.93; P < 0.001) with 46% of eyes experiencing vision loss at 5 years (with or without progression to late AMD).

DPED was associated with increased risk of progression to late AMD (hazard ratio [HR], 2.36; 95% confidence interval [CI], 1.98-2.82; P < 0.001); 67% of eyes progressed to late AMD 5 years after DPED detection.
This study replicates the results of previous natural history studies of eyes with DPED including the high rates of progression to late AMD and vision loss (regardless of progression to late AMD).
Persons with large drusen or with pigmentary abnormalities associated with at least medium drusen should be considered to have intermediate AMD.

Five-year risks of progressing to late AMD are estimated to increase approximately 100 fold, ranging from a 0.5% 5-year risk for normal aging changes to a 50% risk for the highest intermediate AMD risk group.

claim: In Asian adults with symptomatic macular polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy, ranibizumab plus verteporfin photodynamic therapy produced better improvement in best-corrected visual acuity than ranibizumab monotherapy after 12 months.
support_summary: The strongest direct support comes from the Japanese EVEREST II subanalysis, a randomized study in Asian patients with symptomatic macular PCV, where 12-month BCVA gain was higher with ranibizumab plus verteporfin PDT than with ranibizumab alone (+8.5 vs +6.4 letters), alongside much higher complete polyp regression and fewer injections (PMID: 30271112). Additional PCV studies also report better visual improvement with ranibizumab-plus-PDT versus ranibizumab monotherapy, though they are less directly aligned because they are retrospective, do not always specify verteporfin, or emphasize 36-month rather than 12-month outcomes (PMID: 27237048, PMID: 29434739). The 24-month main EVEREST II trial in Asian participants further supports superiority of combination therapy for BCVA gain, though its primary evidence is at 24 months rather than the claim's 12-month timepoint (PMID: 32672800). Overall, the support side consistently favors combination therapy, with the most claim-specific evidence coming from EVEREST II-related data.
contradict_summary: Several contradiction items are only indirectly relevant or miscategorized because they study neovascular age-related macular degeneration or myopic choroidal neovascularization rather than PCV, so they should not be weighed heavily against this PCV-specific claim (PMID: 20577273, PMID: 21124254, PMID: 30204730, PMID: 23876867). Among the directly relevant PCV studies, some Korean and other prospective/retrospective cohorts found no statistically significant 12-month BCVA difference between ranibizumab with versus without PDT, even when polyp regression favored combination therapy (PMID: 21757883, PMID: 21610566, PMID: 29925341). Thus, the main contradictory theme is not that monotherapy is clearly superior in PCV, but rather that some smaller non-EVEREST PCV studies reported similar visual acuity outcomes at 12 months despite anatomical advantages for combination therapy. This creates some heterogeneity, but the contradiction is weakened by small sample sizes and off-target disease populations in several cited articles.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. A randomized Asian PCV trial and its Japanese 12-month subanalysis directly show greater BCVA improvement with ranibizumab plus verteporfin PDT than ranibizumab monotherapy, whereas the opposing PCV evidence mainly consists of smaller studies showing no significant difference rather than clear superiority of monotherapy, and several contradiction items are from different diseases.

claim: In Asian adults with symptomatic macular polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy, ranibizumab plus verteporfin photodynamic therapy achieved higher complete polyp regression than ranibizumab monotherapy after 12 months.
support_summary: Multiple studies support that adding verteporfin photodynamic therapy to ranibizumab increases complete polyp regression in symptomatic macular polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy. The strongest direct evidence is the Japanese EVEREST II cohort, in which complete polyp regression at 12 months was 70.5% with combination therapy versus 27.3% with ranibizumab monotherapy (PMID: 30271112). Additional EVEREST II-derived analyses also found higher odds or rates of complete polyp regression at month 12 with combination therapy, including an adjusted odds ratio of 4.64 for complete regression (PMID: 35963005) and 80.0% versus 35.7% regression in a lesion-evolution substudy (PMID: 36246176). An earlier smaller study likewise reported much higher complete regression with combined ranibizumab/PDT than ranibizumab alone, although its explicit regression comparison was at 3 months rather than 12 months, making it only partially aligned with the claim (PMID: 21610566); the 24-month EVEREST II report also supports the same direction of effect but not the exact 12-month endpoint (PMID: 32672800).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported. This is driven by direct 12-month evidence from Asian/Japanese EVEREST II populations showing substantially higher complete polyp regression with ranibizumab plus verteporfin photodynamic therapy than with ranibizumab monotherapy, with additional supportive but less directly matched studies reinforcing the same pattern.

support_highlights.snippet: At Month 12, mean change in BCVA letter score was +8.5 with combination therapy versus +6.4 with monotherapy.
Complete polyp regression was higher with combination therapy than with monotherapy at Month 12 (70.5% vs 27.3%).
Combination therapy was effective in improving BCVA and achieving a higher rate of complete polyp regression with a lower number of ranibizumab injections than monotherapy.
Complete regression of polypoidal lesions in indocyanine green angiography was found in 1 (14.3%) eye in the ranibizumab group, compared with 15 (93.8%) eyes in the combined group (P = 0.001).
However, combined ranibizumab and PDT appeared to be more effective in causing complete regression of the polypoidal lesions in indocyanine green angiography compared with ranibizumab monotherapy.
Combination therapy was superior to monotherapy in terms of complete polypoidal lesion regression at month 24 (81 of 143 [56.6%] vs 23 of 86 [26.7%] participants; P < .001).

Combination therapy with vPDT added to ranibizumab achieved superior BCVA gain, increased odds of complete polypoidal lesion regression, and fewer treatment episodes compared with ranibizumab monotherapy.

Patients treated with combination therapy had higher odds of achieving CPREG at M12 (adjusted odds ratio = 4.64; 95% confidence interval, 2.85-7.55; P < 0.001) compared with those in the monotherapy group.
At M12, treatment with combination therapy was associated with higher probability of achieving CPREG than with ranibizumab monotherapy.
Complete polypoidal lesion regression was higher in the combination therapy group compared with the monotherapy group at all visits (month 12, 12 of 15 patients [80%] vs. 5 of 14 patients [35.7%];
Combination therapy was associated with higher rates of complete polypoidal lesion regression and fewer persistent polypoidal lesions compared with monotherapy.

claim: In Asian adults with symptomatic macular polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy, ranibizumab plus verteporfin photodynamic therapy required fewer intravitreal ranibizumab injections than ranibizumab monotherapy over 12 months.
support_summary: Direct supporting evidence comes from Asian PCV studies showing lower ranibizumab treatment burden with combination therapy. In the Japanese EVEREST II cohort, over 12 months the combination arm received a median of 4.0 ranibizumab injections versus 7.0 with monotherapy, explicitly supporting the claim in Japanese patients with symptomatic macular PCV (PMID: 30271112). A broader PCV study also reported that combination treatment significantly reduced IVR injection numbers versus monotherapy, although the abstract does not specify verteporfin and the population is not clearly limited to Asian adults with symptomatic macular macular disease as in the claim (PMID: 29434739). The 24-month EVEREST II trial in Asian participants found fewer ranibizumab injections with ranibizumab+vPDT than monotherapy, which is directionally consistent but does not directly address the 12-month time point in the claim (PMID: 32672800).
contradict_summary: Contradicting evidence includes one directly relevant but small Korean PCV study and two less relevant AMD studies. In Korean patients with PCV, a retrospective review found no statistically significant difference in ranibizumab injection numbers between ranibizumab+PDT and ranibizumab alone over 1 year, directly opposing the claim though based on only 24 eyes (PMID: 21757883). Two randomized studies in exudative/neovascular age-related macular degeneration, not PCV, reported that adding verteporfin PDT did not reduce ranibizumab injections at 1 year, so these contradict the general treatment-burden concept but are disease-mismatched relative to the claim (PMID: 20577273; PMID: 20957143). Thus, the main discrepancy is that much of the contradictory evidence comes from AMD rather than Asian adults with symptomatic macular PCV.
conclusion: The claim is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest and most directly relevant evidence is the Japanese EVEREST II 12-month PCV analysis showing fewer injections with ranibizumab plus verteporfin PDT, while contradictory evidence is either small and retrospective in PCV or comes from AMD populations that do not match the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: Over 12 months, patients in the combination arm received a median of 4.0 ranibizumab injections vs 7.0 in the monotherapy arm.
Combination therapy was effective in improving BCVA and achieving a higher rate of complete polyp regression with a lower number of ranibizumab injections than monotherapy.
In addition, combination treatment may significantly reduce the IVR injection numbers required to treat PCV.
Patients receiving combination treatment with IVR injection and PDT have greater vision improvements, reduced macular degeneration and decreased injection numbers.

Participants in the combination group received fewer ranibizumab injections (median, 6.0 [interquartile range (IQR), 4.0-11.0]) than the monotherapy group (median, 12.0 [IQR, 7.0-17.0]) up to month 24.

Combination therapy with vPDT added to ranibizumab achieved superior BCVA gain, increased odds of complete polypoidal lesion regression, and fewer treatment episodes compared with ranibizumab monotherapy.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Both groups required a mean of 1.3 injections of ranibizumab following the 3-month loading phase.
The addition of standard-fluence verteporfin PDT at baseline to a ranibizumab regimen conferred no benefit in terms of visual acuity or number of ranibizumab injections required at 1 year.
Central retinal thickness decreased in six of seven patients, but ranibizumab injections did not decrease.

Despite hypoperfusion, no decrease in visual acuity or injections required was noted, suggesting that even higher fluence levels of vPDT may be necessary to decrease the number of anti-VEGF injections.
The number of ranibizumab injections was not significantly different either (p = 0.555).

claim: In participants with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, intravitreous lampalizumab did not reduce enlargement of geographic atrophy compared with sham procedure during treatment.
support_summary: No supporting evidence items were provided.
contradict_summary: One contradicting item suggests lampalizumab may reduce geographic atrophy progression by targeting complement factor D, based on the MAHALO phase 2 trial described in the abstract (PMID: 28637922). However, the provided abstract is truncated and mainly frames lampalizumab as a potential therapy and describes the trial background, limiting how confidently efficacy can be inferred directly from the abstract text. Thus, while this item is directionally contradictory to the claim, the abstract-level evidence is limited.
conclusion: The statement is weakly contradicted by the available related evidence. This judgment is based on a single, apparently positive phase 2 lampalizumab study (PMID: 28637922), but the abstract provided is incomplete and offers limited direct abstract-level confirmation of reduced geographic atrophy enlargement.

claim: In participants with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, lampalizumab showed no benefit in prespecified subgroups, including subgroups defined by complement factor I-profile genetic biomarker.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In participants with geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, intravitreous lampalizumab had a low rate of serious injection-related adverse events such as endophthalmitis.
support_summary: No non-zero supporting evidence items were available.
contradict_summary: No non-zero contradicting evidence items were available.
conclusion: This statement had only zero-score or missing-score rows in the input file.

claim: In children with juvenile-onset myopia, daily disposable MiSight soft contact lenses slowed progression of myopia compared with daily disposable Proclear soft contact lenses, as shown by less worsening of cycloplegic spherical equivalent refraction.
support_summary: One multicenter clinical trial extension directly supports the claim by reporting that MiSight 1 day dual-focus lenses slowed juvenile-onset myopia progression relative to the original single-vision Proclear 1 day control during the initial randomized 3-year phase, with cycloplegic spherical equivalent refractive error monitored as an outcome (PMID: 35086120). The 6-year follow-up showed that the earlier treatment benefit was sustained in children originally assigned to MiSight, and children switched from Proclear to MiSight later also showed slowed progression (PMID: 35086120). Although the abstract for the extension emphasizes long-term maintenance and post-switch outcomes more than the original between-group 3-year SER difference, its description of part 1 as a comparison against Proclear is consistent with the claim (PMID: 35086120).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The available evidence includes a relevant multicenter clinical trial follow-up directly tied to the original MiSight-versus-Proclear comparison and indicates sustained slowing of myopia progression with MiSight, while no contradictory evidence was provided.

support_highlights.snippet: Treatment of myopic children with a dual-focus soft contact lens (DFCL; MiSight 1 day) produced sustained slowing of myopia progression over a 6-year period.
Part 1 was a 3-year clinical trial comparing DFCLs with a control contact lens (Proclear 1 day) at four investigational sites.
Cyclopleged spherical equivalent refractive errors (SEREs) and axial lengths (ALs) were monitored, and a linear mixed model was used to compare their adjusted change annually.
CONCLUSIONS: Dual-focus soft contact lenses continue to slow the progression of myopia in children over a 6-year period revealing an accumulation of treatment effect.

claim: In children with juvenile-onset myopia, daily disposable MiSight soft contact lenses slowed eye elongation compared with daily disposable Proclear soft contact lenses, as shown by less increase in axial length.
support_summary: One multicenter clinical trial follow-up directly supports the claim by stating that Part 1 was a 3-year comparison of MiSight 1 day dual-focus contact lenses versus Proclear 1 day control lenses, and that the slowed progression seen with MiSight was sustained over 6 years (PMID: 35086120). The abstract specifically reports reduced axial length growth with continued MiSight wear and notes that children switched from the original Proclear control to MiSight later showed a 71% slowing in eye growth, which is consistent with less axial elongation under MiSight treatment (PMID: 35086120). However, this abstract is a long-term extension and does not restate the original between-group Part 1 axial-length difference numerically in the abstract itself, so support for the exact direct MiSight-versus-Proclear comparison comes partly from the study design/context rather than fully detailed head-to-head abstract results (PMID: 35086120).
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall supported. The available evidence includes a direct extension of the original MiSight versus Proclear trial showing sustained slowing of myopia progression and reduced axial elongation with MiSight, although the abstract provides stronger long-term treatment evidence than detailed original head-to-head axial-length numbers.

support_highlights.snippet: Treatment of myopic children with a dual-focus soft contact lens (DFCL; MiSight 1 day) produced sustained slowing of myopia progression over a 6-year period.
Part 1 was a 3-year clinical trial comparing DFCLs with a control contact lens (Proclear 1 day) at four investigational sites.

Slowed myopia progression in the T6 group observed during part 1 was sustained throughout part 2 (mean ± standard error of the mean: change from baseline SERE [in diopters], -0.52 ± 0.076 vs. -0.51 ± 0.076; change in AL [in millimeters], 0.28 ± 0.033 vs. 0.23 ± 0.033; both P > .05).
Within-eye comparisons of AL growth revealed a 71% slowing for the T3 group (3 years older than part 1) and further revealed a small subset of eyes (10%) that did not respond to treatment.
Dual-focus soft contact lenses continue to slow the progression of myopia in children over a 6-year period revealing an accumulation of treatment effect.
Eye growth of the initial control cohort with DFCL was slowed by 71% over the subsequent 3-year treatment period.

claim: In children with juvenile-onset myopia wearing daily disposable MiSight soft contact lenses or Proclear soft contact lenses, no serious ocular adverse events occurred during the clinical trial.
support_summary: Direct support comes from a 6-year double-masked trial of children aged 8-12 years randomized to MiSight 1 day or Proclear 1 day, which explicitly reported that no contact lens-related adverse events were classified as serious and concluded there were no contact lens-related serious adverse events across follow-up (PMID: 33549474). Additional indirect support comes from broader pediatric soft contact lens studies reporting no serious ocular adverse events in children wearing soft lenses for myopia control, though these did not specifically study both MiSight and Proclear as in the claim (PMID: 35413027, PMID: 38127196). A MiSight-only clinical study in Korean children also reported no observed side effects, but this is short-term and does not address Proclear (PMID: 39191206). Overall, the strongest and most directly relevant evidence is PMID: 33549474; the other studies mainly reinforce the general safety profile of pediatric soft contact lens wear.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided. No abstract here reports serious ocular adverse events occurring in children wearing MiSight or Proclear lenses during the referenced clinical trial.
conclusion: The statement is overall strongly supported because a directly relevant long-term trial of MiSight 1 day and Proclear 1 day in children explicitly found no contact lens-related serious adverse events (PMID: 33549474). The remaining studies provide indirect but consistent safety evidence in pediatric soft contact lens wear, and no contradictory evidence was supplied.

support_highlights.snippet: Only three subjects discontinued due to an ocular adverse event (AE).
No contact lens related AEs were classified as serious.
Across the 6-years, there were no contact lens related serious AEs and biomicroscopy showed no significant changes.
None of the ocular adverse events were serious or severe or caused permanent contact lens discontinuation.

The adverse events experienced by 7- to 11-year-old myopic children rarely required meaningful treatment and never led to permanent discontinuation of contact lens wear or loss of best-corrected vision.
The treatment was well tolerated, and no serious adverse events were observed.
Regardless of Ortho-K history, children showed good visual acuity after wearing MiSight CL, and no other side effects were observed.

claim: In eyes with early or intermediate age-related macular degeneration in the fellow eye, drusen and hyperreflective foci on optical coherence tomography were imaging biomarkers associated with progression to macular neovascularization or macular atrophy.
support_summary: Several longitudinal OCT-based studies support drusen and hyperreflective foci as risk markers for progression from early/intermediate AMD to late disease. Most directly, a 10-year fellow-eye study found that fellow eyes with drusen and intraretinal hyperreflective foci had higher risk of developing exudative AMD, explicitly matching the fellow-eye progression component of the claim (PMID: 39172949). Additional studies in intermediate AMD reported that hyperreflective foci, drusen burden/drusen area or drusenoid PED-related features predicted conversion to neovascular AMD and/or atrophy, including GA/cRORA (PMID: 28715590, PMID: 29971444, PMID: 33009219, PMID: 35174809, PMID: 36228779, PMID: 39689291). Some supporting items are less direct because they are cross-sectional, concern treated neovascular AMD eyes, or do not specifically study fellow eyes, so their relevance is partial rather than exact (PMID: 22968145, PMID: 33221285).
contradict_summary: One study presents partial contradiction by showing that OCT-reflective drusen substructures predicted progression to geographic atrophy but not to choroidal neovascularization in intermediate AMD (PMID: 27793356). However, this does not negate the claim overall because the claim allows progression to either macular neovascularization or macular atrophy, and this study still supports the atrophy part while only disputing one drusen-related pathway to CNV. Thus, the contradiction is limited and focused on a specific drusen phenotype rather than drusen and hyperreflective foci broadly.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from a directly relevant fellow-eye longitudinal study showing drusen and intraretinal hyperreflective foci increased risk of exudative AMD (PMID: 39172949), and multiple additional longitudinal studies in intermediate AMD link these OCT features to neovascular and/or atrophic progression, whereas the lone contradictory study only narrows the association for a specific drusen substructure and only for CNV.

support_highlights.snippet: Fellow eyes with drusen (large HR = 6.938, P = 0.001; cuticular HR = 6.937, P < 0.0001; subretinal drusenoid deposits HR = 13.678, P < 0.0001) and intraretinal hyperreflective foci (HR = 1.853, P = 0.041) were also at higher risk.
Drusen and intraretinal hyperreflective foci were also significant risk factors for macular neovascularization development.
Focal hyperreflectivity over drusen, drusen cores, and hyper- or hyporeflectivity of drusen were also associated with RPE atrophy.

Drusenoid RPE detachment, RPE thickening, and retinal pigmentary hyperreflective material were significantly associated with higher risk of progression to advanced AMD (ORs: 5.0-8.5) and NV (ORs: 10.8-17.2).
Pigmentary hyperreflective material was associated with progression to GA (OR: 7.5, P = 0.009).
The most critical quantitative features for progression were outer retinal thickness, hyperreflective foci, and drusen area.
Predictive hallmarks for CNV were mostly drusen-centric, while GA markers were associated with neurosensory retina and age.
Eyes that progressed were significantly more likely to show intraretinal hyperreflective foci, hyporeflective drusen cores, and drusen volume ≥0.03 mm3.

Intraretinal hyperreflective foci, hyporeflective drusen cores, drusen volume ≥0.03 mm3, and higher CC flow deficits were significantly and independently associated with the development of complete retinal pigment epithelial and outer retinal atrophy.
Among pixels of MA at year 2, 78% were preceded by thick drusen, 54% by subretinal macular neovascularization (MNV), and 22.5% by no detectable OCT features.
Both DPED collapse and progression to neovascular age-related macular degeneration were preceded by the presence of hyperreflective foci over the DPED.
Thick DLS, IHRF, and fellow eye exudative MNV were found to be independent predictors for the development of exudative MNV within 2 years.
Thick DLS, IHRF, and fellow eye exudative MNV were associated with an increased risk of progression from iAMD to exudative MNV.

Multivariate analysis revealed that hyperreflective foci and MNV in the fellow eye were associated with the development of cRORA, and higher PED height was a significant factor associated with the development of MNV.
Notably, patients with this characteristic entity can progress to late AMD, including cRORA and MNV, in a significant proportion of cases.

contradict_highlights.snippet: The ODS at baseline were associated with (1) greater macular drusen volume at baseline (P < 0.001), (2) development of preatrophic changes at year 2 (P = 0.001-0.01), and (3) development of macular GA (P = 0.005) and preatrophic changes at year 3 (P = 0.002-0.008), but not development of CNV.
Optical coherence tomography-reflective drusen substructures are optical coherence tomography-based biomarkers of progression to GA, but not to CNV, in eyes with intermediate AMD.

claim: Eyes that progressed to macular neovascularization or macular atrophy showed distinct topographic patterns of drusen and hyperreflective foci over time compared with eyes that did not progress to advanced age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: The supporting studies overall suggest that specific drusen-related and hyperreflective OCT features are associated with progression to advanced AMD, with some evidence that neovascular and atrophic pathways have distinct spatial or feature patterns. The strongest direct support comes from an OCT/AI longitudinal study showing that hyperreflective foci and drusen area were among the most critical progression features, and that conversion patterns differed between neovascular and atrophic outcomes, with neovascular progression being more drusen-centric (PMID: 29971444). Additional longitudinal and imaging-pathology studies report that hyperreflective foci over drusenoid PED preceded collapse or neovascular AMD and that thick drusen often preceded later macular atrophy, supporting a link between local lesion topology and later advanced disease (PMID: 35174809; PMID: 33221285). Broader conceptual and histopathologic papers also indicate that deposit type and macular topography are related to future disease course, including links between subretinal drusenoid deposits, type 1 macular neovascularization risk, and regional macular distribution, though these do not directly compare progressors versus non-progressors over time (PMID: 28557901; PMID: 33512402). A notable discrepancy is that several SUPPORT items are only indirect evidence and do not explicitly analyze the exact topographic evolution of drusen and hyperreflective foci in progressing versus non-progressing eyes.
contradict_summary: No contradicting evidence items were provided.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. Support is driven mainly by longitudinal OCT evidence showing distinct progression-associated patterns for drusen and hyperreflective foci, especially differing between neovascular and atrophic pathways, although much of the remaining evidence is indirect rather than an exact match to the claim.

support_highlights.snippet: The deposition of specific types of deposit seems to be highly correlated with choroidal thickness and topographical location in the macula.
The particular type of extracellular deposit is predictive of the future course of the patient.

For example, eyes with subretinal drusenoid deposits have a propensity to develop outer retinal atrophy, complete outer retinal and retinal pigment epithelial atrophy, or Type 3 neovascularization as specific forms of late AMD.
The most critical quantitative features for progression were outer retinal thickness, hyperreflective foci, and drusen area.
The features for conversion showed pathognomonic patterns that were distinctly different for the neovascular and the atrophic pathways.
Predictive hallmarks for CNV were mostly drusen-centric, while GA markers were associated with neurosensory retina and age.
Moreover, pathways of progression may be specific in respect to the neovascular/atrophic type.

Retinal pigment epithelium atrophy and photoreceptor loss on OCT were highly predictive of MA at that location at years 2 and 5 (P < .0001), but accounted for only 22.5% of the ensuing atrophy at year 2 and less at year 5.
Among pixels of MA at year 2, 78% were preceded by thick drusen, 54% by subretinal macular neovascularization (MNV), and 22.5% by no detectable OCT features.
In the fovea, BLinD/drusen was prevalent in AMD eyes; pre-BLinD was prevalent in control eyes.
SDD was low in the fovea and high in perifovea, especially in AMD eyes.
Although invisible, BLinD may presage type 1 MNV.
Deposit topography suggests one outer retinal lipid recycling system serving specialized cone and rod physiology, and its dysregulation in AMD is due to impaired transfer to the circulation.
Both DPED collapse and progression to neovascular age-related macular degeneration were preceded by the presence of hyperreflective foci over the DPED.
The natural history of DPED comprises collapse (sometimes followed by the development of atrophy), vascularization followed by exudation, or DPED persistence.

Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography can confirm retinal pigment epithelial elevation caused by drusenoid accumulation and facilitate the identification of high-risk features that correlate with progression.

claim: Automated localization and quantification of drusen and hyperreflective foci may improve prediction of future disease progression in age-related macular degeneration.
support_summary: Several studies support that OCT-derived drusen and hyperreflective foci (HRF) are useful progression biomarkers in AMD, and some directly show benefit from automated/AI-based quantification. Most directly, machine-learning models using automated segmentation of drusen and HRF predicted drusen regression or conversion to advanced AMD with moderate-to-good performance, and identified HRF/drusen measurements among the most important predictors (PMID: 28658477; PMID: 29971444). Multiple longitudinal or population-based studies also found that HRF, drusen volume/area, or drusenoid PED measurements were independently associated with later neovascular AMD, geographic atrophy, or late AMD, supporting the biological relevance of quantifying these features for risk stratification even when automation was not the central focus (PMID: 28166160; PMID: 33009219; PMID: 33348086; PMID: 36079043). Additional studies describe automated methods to localize/quantify drusen or HRF and frame these features as progression-associated biomarkers, though some are more about detection, function, or pathobiology than prediction per se (PMID: 24111453; PMID: 36032584; PMID: 37170011; PMID: 36755326). A few SUPPORT items are only indirect support because they show association with disease severity or progression markers rather than demonstrating that automation itself improves predictive accuracy (e.g., PMID: 19167082; PMID: 31036867).
contradict_summary: One study specifically tested whether adding OCT-defined HRF that lacked corresponding hyperpigmentary abnormalities on color photos improved prediction of late AMD, and found that it did not improve predictive performance beyond models including HPA extent, drusen volume, and age (PMID: 37831941). However, the same study still found HRF extent independently associated with reduced visual sensitivity, so the contradiction is narrow: it challenges incremental prognostic value for this particular HRF subtype/model rather than disproving the broader usefulness of automated drusen/HRF quantification overall.
conclusion: The statement is overall more supported than contradicted. The strongest evidence comes from studies directly using automated OCT segmentation and machine learning to predict AMD progression and identifying drusen/HRF features as key predictors (PMID: 28658477; PMID: 29971444), whereas the single contradictory study only shows lack of added predictive value for a specific HRF subset in one modeling context (PMID: 37831941).

support_highlights.snippet: Segmentation of drusen and the overlying layers was obtained using a graph-theoretic method, and the hyperreflective foci were segmented using a voxel classification method.
Finally, a machine learning method based on a sparse Cox proportional hazard regression was developed to estimate a risk score and predict the incoming regression of individual drusen.
Prediction within the first 2 years achieved an AUC of 0.75.
The predictive model proposed in this study represents a promising step toward image-guided prediction of AMD progression.
We obtained automated volumetric segmentation of outer neurosensory layers and retinal pigment epithelium, drusen, and hyperreflective foci by spectral domain-OCT image analysis.
Using imaging, demographic, and genetic input features, we developed and validated a machine learning-based predictive model assessing the risk of conversion to advanced AMD.
Our predictive model differentiated converting versus nonconverting eyes with a performance of 0.68 and 0.80 for CNV and GA, respectively.
The most critical quantitative features for progression were outer retinal thickness, hyperreflective foci, and drusen area.
Artificial intelligence with automated analysis of imaging biomarkers allows personalized prediction of AMD progression.
Quantitative imaging biomarkers may aid in defining risk of disease progression.
The hyperreflective foci overlying drusen are likely to represent progression of disease RPE cell migration into the retina and possible photoreceptor degeneration or glial scar formation.
Quantification of drusen size and total area covered by drusen is an important risk factor for progression.
In this paper, we propose a method to detect drusen and quantify drusen size along with the area covered with drusen in macular region from standard color retinal images.
Hyperreflective foci represented the single most important predictor of neovascular conversion (Exp [B], 15.15; P = 0.005) as confirmed by Kaplan-Meier curve (P = 0.002).

Drusenoid pigment epithelial detachment width was significantly greater in NVAMD group than control subjects at baseline and last follow-up (P < 0.001), and its delta value also resulted a significant neovascular predictor (Exp [B], 0.99; P = 0.04).
Hyperreflective foci significantly increase the risk of NVAMD progression.
The delta width of drusenoid pigment epithelial detachment also predicts disease progression, integrating the stratification of NVAMD progression risk.

Several prediction models for progression of age-related macular degeneration (AMD) have been developed, but the added value of using genetic information in those models in addition to clinical characteristics is ambiguous.
in addition to the clinical severity grading at baseline as quantified by validated drusen detection software, to predict disease progression in 177 AMD patients after 6.5 years follow-up.
The GRS was strongly associated with the drusen coverage at baseline (P < 0.001) and both the GRS and drusen coverage were associated with disease progression.
Eyes that progressed were significantly more likely to show intraretinal hyperreflective foci, hyporeflective drusen cores, and drusen volume ≥0.03 mm3.

Intraretinal hyperreflective foci, hyporeflective drusen cores, drusen volume ≥0.03 mm3, and higher CC flow deficits were significantly and independently associated with the development of complete retinal pigment epithelial and outer retinal atrophy.

In macula-wide analyses, spectral-domain (SD) optical coherence tomography (OCT) features including drusen volume, hyperreflective foci, and OCT-reflective drusen substructures independently predict geographic atrophy (GA) onset secondary to age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

Compared with paired control regions, GA precursor regions at 2, 3, and 4 years before (n = 54, 33, and 25, respectively) showed greater drusen volume (P = 0.01, P = 0.003, and P = 0.003, respectively).

At 2 and 3 years before GA onset, they were associated with the presence of hypertransmission (P < 0.001 and P = 0.03, respectively), hyperreflective foci (P < 0.001 and P = 0.045, respectively), OCT-reflective drusen substructures (P = 0.004 and P = 0.03, respectively), and loss or disruption of the photoreceptor zone, ellipsoid zone, and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE, P < 0.001 and P = 0.005-0.045, respectively).

An automated depth-resolved algorithm using optical attenuation coefficients (OACs) was developed to visualize, localize, and quantify hyperreflective foci (HRF) seen on OCT imaging that are associated with macular hyperpigmentation and represent an increased risk of disease progression in age related macular degeneration.
HRF were visualized and localized within the entire scan by differentiating HRF within the retina from HRF along the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE).

Univariate logistic regression showed that drusen area and volume in both 3 mm and 5 mm circles, subfoveal choroidal thickness, drusen volume ≥ 0.03 mm3 in the 3 mm ring, SDD, IHRF, and hDC were all associated with an increased risk for development of late AMD.

The multivariate regression model identified that drusen volume in the 3 mm ring (OR: 2.59, p = 0.049) and presence of IHRF (OR: 57.06, p < 0.001) remained as independent and significant risk factors for progression to late AMD.
This population-based study confirms previous findings from clinic-based studies that high central drusen volume and IHRF are associated with an increased risk of progression to late AMD.
These findings may be of value in risk-stratifying patients in clinical practice or identifying subjects for early intervention clinical trials.

Retinal melanosome/melanolipofuscin-containing cells (MCCs), clinically visible as hyperreflective foci (HRF) and a highly predictive imaging biomarker for the progression of age-related macular degeneration (AMD), are widely believed to be migrating retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells.
The volume and mean thickness of drusen and HRF within a circle of 240 µm centred at each stimulus point was determined using automated AI-based image segmentation algorithms.
Biomarker quantification using AI-methods allows to determine the impact of sub-clinical features in the progression of AMD.

contradict_highlights.snippet: Predictive models for progression to late age-related macular degeneration, accounting for drusen volume and age, were developed using HPA extent, with and without HRF (OCT+/CFP-) extent.
Incorporating HRF (OCT+/CFP-) extent did not improve the predictive performance for late age-related macular degeneration development ( P ≥ 0.32).
The addition of HRF (OCT+/CFP-) extent to HPA extent did not improve the prediction of late age-related macular degeneration development.
